contents: Non-controlling interest (also called minority interest) represents the portion of a subsidiary's equity that belongs to shareholders other than the parent company. On the consolidated balance sheet, it is reported to show the claim on the subsidiary's assets belonging to these non-controlling shareholders.
The fourth season of Chicago Fire contained 23 episodes. [3]
The Eagles sing "Love Will Keep Us Alive," with Timothy B. Schmit providing the lead vocals.
Patrick Brown was elected leader of the Ontario PC Party in May 2015.

"Fishin' in the Dark" is a song by The Nitty Gritty Dirt Band, featured on their album Hold On. Released as a single in 1987, it became a number-one hit on both the U.S. and Canadian country charts and has sold over a million digital copies, achieving Platinum certification.

Converting a stereo signal to a mono signal is called creating a dual mono signal, which is achieved by panning both the left and right channels equally to both the left and right outputs of an audio mixer.

To prove disparate impact under Title VII, you must first establish that a facially neutral employment practice has a disproportionately adverse effect on members of a protected class compared to non-members. This demonstration of an unjustified adverse impact is the initial requirement before the burden shifts to the employer to justify the practice with business necessity. Intentional discrimination does not need to be shown at this initial stage.

In the present day, Kate lives in Los Angeles, Randall and his family live in New Jersey, and Kevin lives in New York City (after moving from Los Angeles). The frequent flashbacks, however, are set primarily in Pittsburgh where the family lived when the children were young.

In a market economy, decisions about what to produce (investment, production, and distribution) are primarily made through capital and financial markets based on the interplay of supply and demand, which determines prices. This is distinct from a planned economy where a state or controlling body makes these decisions.
Construction began in 2013. The north span opened in August and October 2017. Both spans of the new Tappan Zee Bridge are expected to be fully operational by June 15, 2018.
The song "Can't Help Falling in Love" was originally recorded by Elvis Presley.
Christopher Lloyd plays Emmett "Doc" Brown in the Back to the Future trilogy.
Cigarette advertising on television was banned starting January 2, 1971, as mandated by the Public Health Cigarette Smoking Act passed by Congress in 1970.

The atomic bomb dropped by the United States on Hiroshima on August 6, 1945, was named Little Boy. It was a uranium gun-type bomb delivered by a B-29 Superfortress as part of the Manhattan Project. This bombing, followed three days later by the "Fat Man" plutonium bomb on Nagasaki, caused immediate devastation and resulted in hundreds of thousands of deaths over the following months, predominantly among civilians.
The United States formally took over Wake Island on January 17, 1899, when Commander Edward D. Taussig, acting under orders from President William McKinley, landed and claimed possession for the US.

The American two-party system began to emerge during the Second Party System, starting with the split of the Democratic-Republican Party into factions (Adams' Men and Jackson's Men) in the 1824 election. This division solidified in 1828 with the formation of Andrew Jackson's modern Democratic Party and John Quincy Adams' National Republicans, establishing the foundation for the modern two-party dynamic, though the specific parties (Democrats and Republicans) stabilized later.

Motile sperm cells swim using a flagellum in a water medium to reach the egg. Their energy for movement comes from metabolizing fructose in the seminal fluid, and this energy production occurs in the mitochondria located in the sperm's midpiece, which is found at the base of the sperm head.
An equilateral triangle has 3 lines of symmetry (also called lines of reflection).
There are four seasons of The O.C., as the passage explicitly states that the season described is the "fourth and final season".

The poet presents death as a voyage through the extended metaphor of "crossing the bar," which represents the serene and secure transition from life into death. This metaphorical voyage is guided by the "Pilot," representing God, whom the speaker anticipates meeting face-to-face, emphasizing the divine guidance present throughout life's journey and into death.

The song "Pumped Up Kicks" is about a troubled and delusional youth with homicidal thoughts, warning potential victims to run faster than his bullet. Written by Foster the People's Mark Foster, it aims to explore the psychology behind rising teenage mental illness and youth gun violence, bringing awareness to this epidemic which he attributes to lack of family, lack of love, and isolation. The title refers to the expensive shoes worn as status symbols by the narrator's peers.
States impose protectionist policies primarily to shield domestic producers, businesses, and workers in industries that compete with imports from foreign competitors.

The new Raiders stadium is being built in Paradise, Nevada, specifically on about 62 acres west of Mandalay Bay at Russell Road and Hacienda Avenue, between Polaris Avenue and Dean Martin Drive, just west of Interstate 15.

The event that provoked Congress to propose the Eleventh Amendment and the states to ratify it was the Supreme Court's ruling in *Chisholm v. Georgia* (1793), which held that federal courts could hear lawsuits brought by private citizens against states, denying states sovereign immunity in such cases.
The name "Comanche" originates from the Ute word "kɨmantsi," which translates to "enemy."
Saint Peter, who guards the gates, holds the keys to the kingdom, and determines who is fit to enter heaven by looking up their name in a book.

Bulls on farms are primarily used for natural breeding with cows to maintain herds. Additionally, they are kept specifically to produce semen for profitable sale and use in artificial insemination (AI) programs, which allow farmers to access high-quality genetics safely and efficiently.

A single layer perceptron (true perceptron) is a single neuron using a threshold activation function (like the Heaviside step), strictly performing binary classification. In contrast, a multilayer perceptron (MLP) is a network composed of *many* interconnected neurons organized into layers. While inspired by perceptrons, MLP neurons are not true perceptrons; they can use arbitrary activation functions, enabling them to perform not only classification but also regression tasks. Structurally, an MLP has multiple layers of these neurons, whereas a single layer perceptron has just one output layer.
The Royal Society for the Protection of Birds (RSPB) has more than a million members. This total includes 195,000 youth members.
Percy Faith released the instrumental single "Theme from A Summer Place" in September 1959, prior to the November 1959 release of the film *A Summer Place*.
Jenny Humphrey returns to the Upper East Side for her brother Dan Humphrey's wedding to Serena van der Woodsen, which occurs during a five-year time jump in the final episode of the series.

Athens emerged as the dominant economic power and wealthiest Greek city-state around the late 6th century BCE, primarily due to the discovery of silver deposits in neighbouring mountains and the establishment of an efficient trading system with other city-states, where pottery (evidenced by over 80,000 recovered amphorae) was the main traded product.

Fred Morrison invented the flying disc, creating the first design (Whirlo-Way) in 1946 and later developing the foundational Pluto Platter with Lu in 1955. He sold the rights to Wham-O in 1957. The toy got its name "Frisbee" because Wham-O learned that college students in the Northeast were already calling the Pluto Platter by that name and adopted it alongside the original name.
Lorraine Toussaint played the character Yvonne "Vee" Parker on the Netflix series *Orange Is the New Black*.

The Canadian prime minister does not have a fixed term of office. Once appointed, the prime minister remains in office until they resign, are dismissed by the governor general, or die. While the term of a parliament (and thus the potential lifespan of a government) is constitutionally limited to five years and was amended in 2007 to typically limit majority governments to four years between elections, the prime minister's personal tenure in office is not defined by these time limits.
Willow and Tara tentatively get back together in the episode "Entropy" and their reconciliation continues through the following episode, "Seeing Red".

The performers who sang in the movie *Walk the Line* were Joaquin Phoenix (as Johnny Cash, 9 songs), Reese Witherspoon (as June Carter Cash, 4 songs), Waylon Payne (as Jerry Lee Lewis, 1 song), Johnathan Rice (as Roy Orbison, 1 song), Tyler Hilton (as Elvis Presley, 2 songs), and Shooter Jennings (as Waylon Jennings, 1 song).
After leaving Carthage, Aeneas went to Sicily, then to the western coast of Italy, and finally descended into the underworld.

The passage details the political battle over renewing the military draft during the Vietnam War, culminating in its approval in September 1971 despite a filibuster attempt. Crucially, the last men were conscripted in December 1972 (born in 1952, reporting in June 1973). In early 1973, Secretary of Defense Melvin Laird announced no further draft orders would be issued. Although draft priority numbers were assigned for men born in 1954-1956 as a contingency, the draft was never extended after 1972.

The capital of the Habsburg Empire was primarily located in Vienna. However, it was temporarily moved to Prague from 1583 to 1611. This applies to the Habsburg Monarchy (1521-1918), which was later formally known as the Austrian Empire (1804-1867) and the Austro-Hungarian Empire (1867-1918).
Domhnall Gleeson portrays General Armitage Hux in *Star Wars: The Last Jedi*, as he did in *The Force Awakens*.

The main Winnie-the-Pooh story books were written by A.A. Milne and published as follows: *Winnie-the-Pooh* in 1926 and *The House at Pooh Corner* in 1928. Additionally, poems featuring the bear appeared in *When We Were Very Young* (1924) and *Now We Are Six* (1927).

The electron transport chain, located in the mitochondrial inner membrane, uses energy released as electrons pass from donors (like NADH and FADH2) to acceptors (ultimately oxygen) to actively pump protons (H+) from the mitochondrial matrix into the intermembrane space. This creates a proton gradient across the inner membrane, which is used to drive ATP synthesis.
The theme song for "Mission: Impossible" was written and composed by Argentine composer Lalo Schifrin for the original 1966 TV series.
The microscope slide is placed on the microscope's stage and held in place by slide clips, slide clamps, or a cross-table mechanism.

The seat of the National Legislature (Congress) was moved to the Capitol building in Washington, D.C., prematurely at President John Adams's urging, holding its first session with both chambers (Senate and House of Representatives) on November 17, 1800. This occurred in the completed north (Senate) wing, while the House wing was still under construction (the House moved into its wing in 1807, and the south wing was completed in 1811).

The Eiffel Tower has three visitor levels: the first level features a restaurant, the second level also has a restaurant, and the top level offers an upper platform observation deck at 276 meters above ground.
The 2010 remake of *True Grit* stars Hailee Steinfeld as Mattie Ross and Jeff Bridges as Rooster Cogburn, along with Matt Damon, Josh Brolin, and Barry Pepper.
The University of Texas at Austin has over 50,000 undergraduate and graduate students.
Isle of Dogs had a limited release in the United States on March 23, 2018, followed by a wide release on April 13, 2018.
A Supreme Court justice writes a dissenting opinion to formally express their disagreement with the majority opinion and the resulting judgment of the court, representing the minority view.

According to the biblical Book of Genesis (verses 2:19-20), which inspired Bob Dylan's song "Man Gave Names to All the Animals", Adam named all the animals and birds. The song's lyrics illustrate this by rhyming animal characteristics with their names, such as naming a pig for its curly tail and a cow because Adam saw milk coming out.
NHL players stay on waivers for exactly 24 hours. The claims process begins at noon Eastern Time and ends 24 hours later. During this period, other teams can submit a claim for the player.
The Price Is Right first aired in 1956, premiering on NBC's daytime schedule on November 26th of that year.
Louis Prima sings "I Wan'na Be like You" from the 1967 Disney film *The Jungle Book*, with the song written by Robert and Richard Sherman.

Timbuktu flourished under Mansa Musa's control as a major center for trade, entertainment, and education. Its success in trade was significantly aided by its reliable water supply. Mansa Musa heavily taxed goods passing through the city, highlighting its importance as a hub within the wealthy Mali Empire.
The TV series title "Two Pints of Lager and a Packet of Crisps" was inspired by the 1980 hit single of the same name sung by the band Splodgenessabounds.
The Apple iPhone SE was unveiled on March 21, 2016, and released to the public on March 31, 2016.

During Founder's Day, Anna is captured, injected with vervain, and taken by a policeman to the Gilbert building to be burned along with the other captured vampires, as part of John Gilbert's plan to eradicate them (repeating the 1864 plan). She is killed by John Gilbert inside the building before the burning occurs, and her body is burned with the others there.

The debating club in Almora was established in 1871 A.D. by Pt. Buddhiballav Pant. Its successful operation impressed the provincial Governor, Sir William Muir, who advised opening a press and publishing a newspaper. Following this advice, Pt. Pant opened a press and started publishing the weekly magazine Almora Akhbar.
The New England Patriots have played in ten Super Bowl games throughout their franchise history, which is the most appearances by any team.
The first underwater cable beneath the English Channel was laid in 1850, connecting England and France.

The Haunted Mansion Holiday overlay (Nightmare Before Christmas) is open from late-September through early January each year. It closes in late summer for 2.5 weeks to install the overlay and again in early January to remove it.
Lesley Gore sings the famous 1963 version of "It's My Party" that hit #1 on the pop and R&B charts in the United States.

Elements similar to silver (Ag) are the other members of Group 11 (IUPAC) in the periodic table: copper (Cu) and gold (Au). Roentgenium (Rg) is also placed in this group but its chemical similarity is unconfirmed. These elements are collectively known as the coinage metals and share properties like occurring naturally in elemental form.
"You'll Never Walk Alone" was first released in 1945 as part of the Rodgers and Hammerstein musical *Carousel*.

Following the 2010 midterm elections, the Republican Party controlled the House of Representatives with a majority of 242 seats, while the Democratic Party retained control of the Senate, albeit with a reduced majority. This configuration (Republican House, Democratic Senate) defined the 112th Congress (2011-2013), meaning it was in place throughout 2012.

Big Sur has no specific boundaries but is generally defined as the area encompassing the 71-mile segment of California State Route 1 starting near Malpaso Creek by Carmel Highlands in the north and ending near San Carpóforo Creek by San Simeon in the south, along with the entire Santa Lucia mountain range between those points.

Puerto Rico belonged to Spain before the U.S. The U.S. invaded in 1898 during the Spanish-American War, and Spain formally ceded sovereignty over Puerto Rico to the U.S. under the Treaty of Paris, effective April 11, 1899.
The Panic! at the Disco live album "All My Friends We're Glorious: Death of a Bachelor Live" came out on December 15, 2017. It was released as a limited-edition double-vinyl and digital download.
Ashrita Furman holds the Guinness World Record for the most world records, currently possessing more than 191 official records and having set over 600 records since 1979.
Clare Torry performed the non-lexical vocals on "The Great Gig in the Sky".
The name Marilyn originated as a blend of the English names Mary and Lynn.

The Miami Dolphins last appeared in the Super Bowl for Super Bowl XIX, which they lost. This appearance occurred during the 1984 NFL season (game played in Jan 1985). Their most recent Super Bowl win was in Super Bowl VIII (after the 1973 season).

Un unfair labor practice refers to illegal actions taken by employers or unions that violate the US National Labor Relations Act (NLRA) of 1935 and related laws, which are investigated and enforced by the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB).

The passage explains that the NBA rule change starting with the 2006 Draft ended the direct drafting of high school players. Players must now wait at least one year after high school graduation before becoming draft-eligible, though they aren't required to attend college (e.g., playing overseas or in the D-League). Satnam Singh Bhamara (2015) was the first player drafted under this rule as a high school post-graduate. However, Thon Maker is explicitly identified as the last player drafted directly out of high school; he entered the 2016 Draft as a post-graduate from Orangeville Prep in Canada and was selected 10th overall by the Milwaukee Bucks, making him the first high school lottery pick since 2005. While Anfernee Simons and Matur Maker (2018) are mentioned as prospects, they are not confirmed as drafted within the passage.
The War of 1812 was fought between the United States and the United Kingdom, along with their respective allies.

Transduction in the auditory system occurs when hair cells in the inner ear, specifically on the organ of Corti within the cochlea, convert the mechanical energy of sound vibrations (caused by movement of the basilar membrane) into electrical signals (graded receptor potentials).

The Great Wall of China's length is reported differently based on scope. The Ming Dynasty walls measure 8,850 km, while a comprehensive archaeological survey found the entire wall system, including all branches, measures 21,196 km.

The restaurant in Seinfeld is called Monk's, named after jazz pianist Thelonious Monk due to a poster in the writers' office. While the original pilot episode featured a restaurant called Pete's, it was changed to Monk's before the series began.

RON (Research Octane Number) is measured at 600 rpm under standard conditions, while MON (Motor Octane Number) is measured at a higher engine speed (900 rpm) and under more severe conditions (preheated fuel, variable timing) to stress the fuel more. Consequently, the MON of a fuel is typically 8-12 points lower than its RON, though there's no direct formula linking them. Fuel specifications require meeting minimum standards for both RON and MON independently.
The Flume log flume ride at Alton Towers closed in 2015. It had originally opened in 1981, was rethemed in 2004, and was removed in 2016 for redevelopment into the SW8 rollercoaster.
Little Mix won the eighth series of the UK version of *The X Factor* in 2011. They were the first group to ever win the competition.
The Sohrai festival, celebrated by Santal and Oraon communities in Jharkhand and other eastern Indian states, is associated with cattle worship.

The Declaration of Independence was written primarily to announce to the Kingdom of Great Britain that the thirteen American colonies were breaking away and no longer under British rule, and to announce to the world that these colonies now regarded themselves as independent sovereign states forming a new nation, the United States of America.

The passage details the historical discovery that the nucleus is the center of heredity in a cell. Oscar Hertwig's studies on sea urchin fertilization (1877-1878) showed that the sperm nucleus fuses with the egg nucleus, demonstrating that an individual develops from a single nucleated cell and contradicting earlier theories. Hertwig confirmed this in other animals, and Eduard Strasburger found the same for plants in 1884. This work established the nucleus as having an important role in heredity. Although the nucleus's function as the carrier of genetic information became fully clear later with the discovery of mitosis and the rediscovery of Mendel's rules, the experiments by Hertwig and Strasburger identified the nucleus as the central structure for heredity.
The first immigrants, Polynesian settlers, arrived in Hawaii between 124 and 800 CE.
Michael Buffer is the American ring announcer who coined and trademarked the famous catchphrase "Let's get ready to rumble!" used in boxing and wrestling.
The first NFL Thursday Night Football game was played on November 23, 2006, between the Kansas City Chiefs and the Denver Broncos, with the Chiefs winning 19-10.

There are eleven different types of Pokémon cards in the Trading Card Game. This is a simplified system compared to the video games. The types were introduced over time: seven in the Base Set, followed by Darkness and Metal with Pokémon Gold/Silver, Dragon in the Japanese Dragon Selection set, and finally Fairy in the Japanese XY set.
Cyndi Grecco sang the theme song for "Laverne & Shirley", titled "Making Our Dreams Come True".
The name of season 6 of American Horror Story is American Horror Story: Roanoke.
Sir Rowland Hill proposed the very first adhesive postage stamp in 1837.
Cards are typically cut to the right, as the player designated to make the cut is usually the player to the dealer's right.

The first settlers in San Antonio were Spanish explorers led by Governor Martin de Alarcón, who founded the "Villa de Bejar" (San Antonio) on May 5, 1718, at the headwaters of the San Pedro Creek. They established Mission San Antonio de Valero and a presidio nearby, marking the beginning of Spanish colonial development.
The members of the Traveling Wilburys were Bob Dylan, George Harrison, Jeff Lynne, Roy Orbison, and Tom Petty.
The Bee Gees sang "How Can You Mend a Broken Heart" first, releasing it as a single in 1971.

The original host (moderator) of the long-running U.S. game show "What's My Line?" was John Daly. The show aired on CBS from 1950 to 1967, featured celebrity panelists like Dorothy Kilgallen, Arlene Francis, and Bennett Cerf, and involved guessing contestants' occupations or identifying mystery guests. It won several awards.
Pat McCormick played Big Enos Burdette in *Smokey and the Bandit* and its two sequels.

The Federalists and Anti-Federalists compromised by agreeing that the Anti-Federalists in Massachusetts would support ratifying the Constitution in exchange for the Federalists' promise to support adding amendments, specifically a bill of rights, once the Constitution took effect.

Mark Andreessen defines product/market fit as "being in a good market with a product that can satisfy that market," directly answering the query. Additionally, many interpret achieving this fit as creating a minimum viable product that addresses and solves an existing problem or need in that market.

Kidneys for transplants primarily come from deceased donors, but a significant and growing number also come from living donors, with the proportion varying greatly by country (e.g., 47% from living donors in the US vs. 3% in Spain in 2006).

Growth rings in a tree's cross section are formed annually due to seasonal changes in growth speed, with one ring typically representing one year of the tree's life. Therefore, a stem with 50 rings indicates the tree is approximately 50 years old.
A McDonald's McChicken sandwich consists of a toasted wheat bun, a breaded chicken patty, shredded lettuce, and mayonnaise.
The series C ten-shilling notes ceased to be legal tender on 22 November 1970, having been replaced by the fifty pence coin in 1969 as part of decimalisation.
The Golden State Warriors won their first NBA championship in 1947. They have won a total of five NBA championships, with their victories occurring in the years 1947, 1956, 1975, 2015, and 2017.

The phrase "Pop! Goes the Weasel" originated as the title of an extremely popular English dance first described in 1853 ("An Old English Dance"). At that time, it referred only to the dance itself, performed at high-society balls and dance halls, and the tune (similar to today's) only had the words "Pop! Goes the Weasel" associated with it. Words were added to this already well-known tune and dance title later, around 1855/1856.
The Ranjit Sagar Dam, also known as the Thein Dam, was built on the Ravi River by the Government of Punjab. It is located on the border between the Indian states of Jammu & Kashmir and Punjab.

The original Planet of the Apes was filmed primarily in Arizona and California. Specifically: 1. Arizona: Early desert scenes were filmed near the Grand Canyon, the Colorado River, Lake Powell, Glen Canyon, and Page. 2. California (Malibu Creek State Park): Most ape village scenes (interiors and exteriors) were filmed on the Fox Ranch (now within Malibu Creek State Park), northwest of Los Angeles. 3. California (Coastline): The concluding beach scenes were filmed along the coast between Malibu and Oxnard. The iconic Statue of Liberty remains were filmed in a secluded cove at the eastern end of Westward Beach, Malibu (between Zuma Beach and Point Dume).

The average length of stay in hospice care was 57 days in 2004, an increase from 26 days in 1994 and 19 days in 1998. However, this average is skewed by many very short stays; the median stay in 2004 was 22 days and 33% of patients died within 7 days of admission, primarily because physicians often delay referrals until the very end-stages of disease.
The Houston Astros won the American League pennant in 2017 by defeating the New York Yankees in the American League Championship Series.
Both the cardiac (cardiovascular) center and the vasomotor center are found in the medulla oblongata.

The combined population of the Tri-Cities (Kennewick, Pasco, Richland) in Washington was 193,567 at the 2010 Census. The most recent estimate from the Washington State Office of Financial Management (April 1, 2016) is 217,430.

Membership in Phi Beta Kappa is by invitation only. About 10% of Arts and Sciences graduates from institutions with Phi Beta Kappa chapters are invited, mostly in their senior year (a very small number of exceptional juniors may be invited). To be eligible, students must have studied the liberal arts and sciences, demonstrate "good moral character", and typically have grades placing them in the top tenth of their class. Each chapter sets its own specific academic standards. Invited students must pay an initiation fee (historically $50-$90), which their university might cover.
Pasek and Paul composed the music (including several new songs) for the live television musical *A Christmas Story Live!*.

The song "I Think We're Alone Now" was originally performed by Tommy James and the Shondells, who had a hit with it in 1967. It has also been covered successfully by several other artists, most notably Tiffany (who had a number 1 hit with it in 1987), The Rubinoos (1977), and Girls Aloud (2006).

"https://" signifies the HTTPS URI scheme, which is the secure version of HTTP. It instructs the browser to use SSL/TLS encryption to protect the communication traffic between the client and server. While typically only the server is authenticated (via its certificate), this encryption provides security for web transactions.

Net neutrality is the principle that governments should require internet service providers (ISPs) to treat all internet data equally. This means ISPs cannot discriminate, block, throttle, or charge differently based on the user, content, website, platform, application, device type, or communication method. Key things to know are that it mandates equal treatment, prevents ISPs from favoring or penalizing specific online content or services (like offering free services for some or charging extra/slowing down others), and aims to ensure an open internet.
Ariana Clarice Richards played the little girl, Lex Murphy, in Jurassic Park.

Article Two of the United States Constitution establishes the executive branch, defining its role in carrying out and enforcing federal laws and including the President, Vice President, Cabinet, executive departments, independent agencies, and other boards/commissions/committees.
The second half of Vikings season 5 will premiere in 2018.

Spearman correlation is meant to be used instead of Pearson correlation when the relationship between the two variables is monotonic (consistently increasing or decreasing, but not necessarily linear) rather than strictly linear, or when the data is ordinal or not normally distributed, as Spearman assesses monotonic relationships using rank values.

The variable region of the heavy chain is coded for by the V (variable), D (diversity), and J (joining) gene segments. These segments undergo recombination: first D to J (forming DJ), then V to DJ (forming the rearranged VDJ segment), which constitutes the variable region.

Sri Lanka belongs to South Asia, along with Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, Maldives, Nepal, India, and Pakistan. This region is defined by the territories of these eight nations and is served by the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC).
Star Wars Battlefront II was released worldwide on November 17, 2017, for PlayStation 4, Xbox One, and Microsoft Windows.
Although Terry Reid is credited for "Gimme Some Lovin'" in Days of Thunder, the version actually featured in the movie is performed by The Spencer Davis Group.
The fourteenth season of Grey's Anatomy consists of 24 episodes.
The road that connects the tombs is called the "Spirit Way" (Shéndào), also known as the "Sacred Way".

Blue Bell ice cream is primarily sold in the Southern United States, reaching as far west as Las Vegas, as far north as Indianapolis and Denver, and as far east as Richmond, Virginia, covering approximately 20% of the country.

Charles Darwin proposed evolution by natural selection and common descent as the basis of biological development in his 1859 book *On the Origin of Species*. While Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace jointly presented evolutionary theory in 1858, Darwin's detailed work published in 1859 synthesized evidence and introduced the concepts of natural selection and a branching tree of life. This led to the rapid acceptance of evolution, although the specific mechanism of natural selection wasn't widely embraced until the Modern Synthesis in the mid-20th century.

Yes, The Jungle Book stories are primarily set in India, as Kipling lived there. Specific locations mentioned include Seoni (Madhya Pradesh), northern India (for "In the Rukh"), and the Aravalli hills (Rajasthan) in an early manuscript. However, the geographical details are inconsistent (e.g., Seoni's climate doesn't support rainforests, and distances between mentioned places like Udaipur and Seoni are large). Kipling never visited the central Indian areas now associated with the stories, like Kanha or Pench National Parks.
In the C programming language (and the C family of languages), the semicolon acts as a terminator. It is required after every statement to explicitly mark the end of that statement.

The book "Summer of the Monkeys" is set in Oklahoma at the end of the nineteenth century. Jay Berry Lee moves there with his family from Missouri after his grandfather offers them free land. The events of the story, including Jay Berry finding escaped circus monkeys in a nearby river bottom and his attempts to capture them near his grandfather's store, all occur in this Oklahoma setting.

France gifted the Statue of Liberty to the United States to honor the Union victory in the Civil War and the abolition of slavery, celebrating the realization of freedom and democracy in the US. Édouard de Laboulaye, the key proponent, also intended this gift to inspire the French people to pursue their own democracy against their repressive monarchy.
Ellen's new game show, *Ellen's Game of Games*, had its special preview episode on December 18, 2017, and its official series premiere on January 2, 2018.

The Book of Proverbs is a collection of wisdom teachings spanning centuries, serving as a guide for values, moral behavior, the meaning of life, and right conduct. Its core message is that true wisdom begins with the "fear of God" (submission to God's will). It presents Wisdom as fundamental to creation and the divine order; seeking and aligning with this wisdom is the essence and goal of a religious life, leading to human prosperity and order.

Bubble and squeak is a traditional British dish made by shallow-frying leftover vegetables, primarily potatoes and cabbage. It is named for the bubbling and squeaking sounds the cabbage makes during the cooking process.

The primary motor cortex allocates space based on the density of cutaneous motor receptors and the required precision of movement, not the absolute size of the body part. For this reason, the hands and face occupy the greatest portion of the primary motor cortex, having a much larger representation than body parts like the legs.

Active transport uses cellular energy to move essential molecules (like ions, glucose, and amino acids) against a concentration gradient or other resistance, enabling the cell to accumulate high concentrations of these needed substances.

The English Civil War (1642–1651) was fought between the Parliamentarians (Roundheads) and the Royalists (Cavaliers). They were fighting principally over the manner of England's government, specifically the power struggle between the monarchy (King Charles I and later his son Charles II) and Parliament. The first two wars saw supporters of King Charles I fighting the Long Parliament, while the third war involved supporters of King Charles II fighting the Rump Parliament.
The lyrics for the Christmas carol "What Child Is This?" were written by William Chatterton Dix in 1865 while he was recovering from a severe illness.

The walls of a thermos flask are silvered to minimize heat transfer by thermal radiation between the inner and outer flask surfaces across the partial-vacuum gap, reducing heat loss or gain. This works alongside the partial vacuum, which reduces heat conduction and convection.

The Philadelphia Eagles last played in a Super Bowl at Super Bowl XXXIX (39) in Jacksonville after the 2004 season. They earned the trip by dominating the NFC with a 13-3 record and winning playoff games against the Vikings and Falcons. In the Super Bowl, they faced and lost to the New England Patriots by a score of 24-21.
Kareem Abdul-Jabbar won the NBA MVP award a record six times, the most of any player.
Cellulose is primarily used in the primary cell wall of plant cells, where it serves as a crucial structural component.

Tom Selleck was originally cast to play Indiana Jones. Although Steven Spielberg initially suggested Harrison Ford, George Lucas resisted due to concerns about overusing Ford. After an intensive casting process, Lucas and Spielberg selected Selleck. However, CBS refused to release Selleck from his contract for the TV show *Magnum, P.I.*, forcing him to turn down the role.
The First Temple in Jerusalem was built under King Solomon. Construction began in the fourth year of his reign, which, based on conventional dating (c. 970-931 BCE), places the start around 966 BCE.
Earle Hyman played Russell Huxtable, Cliff Huxtable's father, making him the grandfather on *The Cosby Show*.
In English, "shalom" primarily means peace (especially between entities like humans and God, or between nations) and well-being, welfare, or safety (of an individual or group).
Hermann Ebbinghaus was one of the first to chart the course of forgetting over time when he collected data to plot a forgetting curve in 1885.

A company typically decides to split its stock when the market price per share has become very high, making trading unwieldy and potentially deterring small investors, especially if there is a minimum trading parcel size.
The character Walter performs a solo act of whistling in the telethon's finale. This whistling was actually performed by musician Andrew Bird.
Scotland last qualified for the FIFA World Cup in 1998. They have not appeared at the tournament since then.

Andrew Garfield plays the main character, Desmond Doss, in the 2016 biographical war drama film *Hacksaw Ridge*, directed by Mel Gibson. The film portrays Doss's experiences as a pacifist combat medic during World War II who refused to carry a weapon but earned the Medal of Honor.

The Nusaybah family (also referenced as their ancestral forefathers from the Bani Ghanim al-Khazraj) opens the Church of the Holy Sepulchre. This responsibility was entrusted to their ancestor, Sheikh Ghanim ben Ali ben Hussein al-Ansari al-Khazrajy, by Sultan Saladin in 1192, and the family has held the custody of the doors ever since.

The meaning of "Ticket to Ride" is unclear. While the lyrics describe a girl leaving the narrator, interpretations of the title vary significantly. Paul McCartney claimed it referred to a literal British Railways ticket to Ryde. Conversely, John Lennon stated it described the health cards carried by prostitutes in Hamburg (where "ride" was slang for sex). Some interpretations suggest the song is about a woman leaving her boyfriend to become a prostitute.
"How Deep Is Your Love" by the Bee Gees was released as a single in September 1977.

Yes, carbon tax is explicitly described as a type of Pigovian tax in the passage. This is because it addresses the negative externality of greenhouse gas emissions by forcing emitters to bear the social costs they impose. While carbon taxes can be regressive, disproportionately affecting low-income groups, the revenue can potentially be used to offset this impact.
Creatine is naturally produced (biosynthesized) within the human body from the amino acids glycine and arginine.

When iron reacts with oxygen and water/moisture over time, it undergoes oxidation, forming iron oxide (rust). This process involves oxygen combining with the iron atoms at an atomic level, creating the new compound (rust) which weakens the metal's structure. This specific reaction, requiring both oxygen and moisture, is what causes iron to rust.

The song "Who Are You" was written by Pete Townshend after two events: a draining 13-hour meeting with manager Allan Klein, followed immediately by meeting Sex Pistols members Steve Jones and Paul Cook. While often interpreted as a challenge, Townshend states the song is more about the pressures and demands of forming new friendships (like the one with Jones and Cook) than aggression. Roger Daltrey's aggressive vocal delivery (changing "Who, who, who are you..." to "Who the fuck are you...") shifted the song towards a more nihilistic tone than originally written. Jones and Cook became genuine friends who showed concern for Townshend's well-being.

The ball-and-stick model is not a true representation of an ionic compound's structure because the spheres (atoms/ions) are made much smaller than the rod lengths (bonds). This deliberate scaling down allows a clearer view of the atoms and bonds but distorts reality by failing to show the actual space occupied by the ions and the true proximity between them. Consequently, it creates a misleading impression of significant empty space within the ionic lattice, unlike space-filling models which accurately show occupied space.

The Lincoln Highway, one of the earliest US transcontinental automobile highways, begins at Times Square in New York City and ends at Lincoln Park in San Francisco. While its route underwent realignments changing the number of states it passed through (originally 13, later 14), its defined endpoints remained New York City and San Francisco.
Joe Pizzulo and Leeza Miller sang "Never Gonna Let You Go".

Red bone marrow functions as a primary lymphoid organ within the lymphatic system. Its primary function is to generate lymphocytes from immature hematopoietic progenitor cells. It also performs a valve-like function to prevent the backflow of lymphatic fluid.
The Golden Age of India took place during the rule of the Gupta Empire, specifically from approximately 319 to 485 CE.
World Consumer Rights Day is celebrated every year on 15 March.

The purpose of a Jake brake (Jacobs brake or compression release engine brake) is to slow down a vehicle by opening the exhaust valves after the compression cycle in a diesel engine, releasing the compressed air from the cylinders.
The letter "x" sounds like a /z/ when it is the first letter of a word, such as in *xylophone*, *xenophobia*, and *xanthan*. This is the most common pronunciation for words starting with ⟨x⟩.
After achieving victory in North Africa, the Allies immediately proceeded to launch the Italian Campaign.
Annette Strean provided the vocals for Blue Man Group's cover of "I Feel Love" on their 2003 album *The Complex*.

The meaning of the song "Puff the Magic Dragon," according to its authors Leonard Lipton and Peter Yarrow, is about the hardships of growing older and the loss of childhood innocence. They have repeatedly and strongly denied any intended references to drug use, dismissing such interpretations as incorrect and based on "sloppy research".
Google Glass replaces the standard eyeglass lens with a head-up display.
The third season of Stranger Things has been greenlit, but no official release date has been announced. It is expected to be released in either late 2018 or early 2019.
The World War II Memorial in Washington, D.C. is dedicated to Americans who served in the armed forces and as civilians during World War II.

The First Battle of Bull Run (also known as the First Battle of Manassas), fought on July 21, 1861, was the first major battle of the American Civil War. The Confederates won the battle. Union forces were slow to position themselves, allowing Confederate reinforcements to arrive by rail. The battle ended in a Confederate victory, leading to a disorganized retreat by the Union forces.

The term "Helter Skelter" originates from a British amusement-park ride. It became widely known through the Beatles song of the same name on their 1968 White Album. Charles Manson later misinterpreted the song as referring to an impending apocalyptic war.

Andreas Vesalius was a 16th-century Flemish physician who published *De humani corporis fabrica* (On the Fabric of the Human Body), one of the most influential books on human anatomy. He is considered the founder of modern human anatomy.

April Kepner is the only surgical resident who failed her Boards Exams. This failure resulted in hospitals retracting fellowship offers and her being laid off from Seattle Grace Mercy West. The other residents (including Avery, Karev, Meredith, and Yang) passed their exams.

The Battle of Corinth took place in Corinth, Mississippi. It was a siege during the American Civil War (April 29 - May 30, 1862) where Union forces under Major General Henry Halleck captured the town from Confederate forces commanded by General P.G.T. Beauregard.

According to the Constitution, a person must be at least 25 years old to serve in the House of Representatives, along with being an inhabitant of the state they represent and a U.S. citizen for at least the previous seven years.
The Prudential Center in Newark is home to the New Jersey Devils NHL hockey team and the Seton Hall University Pirates men's basketball team.

The Sun is located near the inner rim of the Orion Arm, within the Local Fluff of the Local Bubble, and within the Gould Belt. It is approximately 26,400 light-years (8,090 parsecs) from the Galactic Center.

The country song "Behind Closed Doors" was written by Kenny O'Dell and recorded by Charlie Rich for his 1973 album. It became Rich's first number-one country hit, spent 20 weeks on the country chart, and was also a successful crossover pop hit.
The cold, dry winds blowing from the Himalayas and the Indo-Gangetic Plain over northern India towards the Indian Ocean during winter are called the Northeast Monsoon or the Retreating Monsoon.
The Chiefs played the Tennessee Titans in the playoffs.
Hurricane Charley made landfall near Cayo Costa, Florida as a Category 4 hurricane with winds of 150 mph.

The main types of train cars are passenger cars (or coaches) for carrying people and freight cars (or wagons) for carrying goods, as these are the revenue-earning types. Additionally, some specialized cars exist for the railroad's own use, such as maintenance cars for track work.

River and the Doctor get married on the spot during the crisis at the top of the pyramid, immediately after River expresses her anguish at the idea of having to kill him. The Doctor marries her specifically so he can whisper his true name in her ear, which is needed to restore reality and prevent the universe's destruction.

The first Mad Max movie was released in April 1979. It gained a cult following, won awards, was very profitable, launched the franchise, and led to three sequels: Mad Max 2 (1981), Beyond Thunderdome (1985), and Fury Road (2015).
Toyota started making cars in the US in 1984 via its joint venture with General Motors at the New United Motor Manufacturing Inc. (NUMMI) plant in Fremont, California.

Ronald Reagan was the oldest person ever elected President of the USA, at 73 years and 274 days old when elected to his second term. Donald Trump was the oldest person at their inauguration (70 years, 220 days), but Reagan was older when elected.

Ursula the Sea Witch's appearance was largely inspired by **American actor and drag queen Divine**. While early designs were briefly influenced by actress Rae and later envisioned as a tall, thin manta ray/scorpion fish-inspired character when Elaine Stritch was considered, animator Rob Minkoff's sketch of a "vampy overweight matron" directly reminded producer Howard Ashman of Divine, leading to the character's final iconic look.

The passage indicates that in 1999, civil aircraft-in-flight contributed approximately 2% of global man-made CO2 emissions. It also references a 2007 report suggesting a higher cumulative climate impact closer to 4%, but this figure includes significant non-CO2 effects (like those from flying at high altitudes) in addition to the CO2 emissions. Therefore, for the specific query about CO2 emissions, the figure cited is around 2%.
Roman Reigns won the first-ever seven-man Elimination Chamber match, becoming the number one contender for Brock Lesnar's Universal Championship at WrestleMania 34.
The Marathon chocolate bar changed its name to Snickers in the United Kingdom, the Isle of Man, and Ireland on 19 July 1990.
Montgomery died after sharing his alcohol with the Beast Folk. This led to a violent scuffle outside, during which he, his servant M'ling, and the Sayer of the Law were killed by the Beast Folk.

The development of Florida's railroads, specifically Henry Flagler's line built along the east coast and to Key West, resulted in the growth of towns and the cultivation of farmland along the rail line.

The point of the Second Amendment was to establish an auxiliary right to keep and bear arms, serving the purposes of enabling self-defense, resisting oppression, and fulfilling the civic duty to collectively defend the state, based on English common law and the Bill of Rights of 1689.

The passage defines "content" as the valuable information, its presentation, and added features delivered through a medium, which the end-user derives value from. It explicitly states that within communication theory (citing McLuhan's "The medium is the message"), the "content" is synonymous with the "message", while the delivery channel is the "medium". The medium affects how the user perceives the content/message.

The initial unification of Upper and Lower Egypt took place around 3000 BC, traditionally attributed to King Menes (believed to be the same as Narmer), as evidenced by symbols like the combined pschent crown and depictions like the Narmer Palette.
The new football stadium is proposed for a large parking lot in Atlanta's Vine City neighborhood, which is less than a mile north of the current Georgia Dome location.
The San Jose Sharks currently play their home games at the SAP Center at San Jose, which is also locally known as the Shark Tank.
More than half of an adult human's 206 bones (126 bones) are located in the appendicular skeleton, which includes the bones of the upper extremities (arms) and lower extremities (legs).
Marc Anthony co-wrote and produced the song "My Baby You" for his album *Marc Anthony*, and it was dedicated to his daughter, Arianna. It was the album's first single without a Spanish version.
"Love Me, I'm a Liberal" is a satirical song by protest singer Phil Ochs that mocks liberals for the contradiction between their stated progressive beliefs and their actual actions or lack thereof.
The music for the musical *The Phantom of the Opera* was written by Andrew Lloyd Webber.

William Jennings Bryan represented the state of Tennessee (the prosecution) in the Scopes Trial, arguing against the teaching of evolution, while Clarence Darrow represented the defense. The trial drew national attention, publicizing the conflict between Fundamentalists and Modernists over evolution and religion, and whether modern science should be taught in schools. Although Scopes was found guilty, the verdict was later overturned.
Kermit the Frog, Jim Henson's famous Muppet character created in 1955, serves as a protagonist in numerous Muppet productions, most notably including Sesame Street and The Muppet Show.

The Red River, also known as the Red River of the South, is a major river in the southern United States. It was named for the red-bed country (soil) within its watershed. Although formerly a tributary of the Mississippi River, it is now a tributary of the Atchafalaya River, connected to the Mississippi via the Old River Control Structure.

The British RAF Fighter Command won the Battle of Britain, achieving a great victory by preventing Germany from knocking Britain out of the war and marking the first major defeat of Hitler's military forces.

A Political Action Committee (PAC) is an organization that pools campaign contributions from its members and uses those funds to support or oppose candidates for elected office, ballot initiatives, or specific pieces of legislation. Its primary function is to influence elections and legislative outcomes through financial donations.

The Tiber River is the river associated with the city of Rome. It is famously known as the main watercourse of Rome, and the city was founded on its eastern banks. The Tiber is Italy's third-longest river, flowing from the Apennine Mountains to the Tyrrhenian Sea.

Former presidents currently receive lifetime Secret Service protection, reinstated by the 2013 Former Presidents Protection Act. This follows a period (1994-2013) where protection was limited to 10 years for presidents inaugurated after 1997, while those before (like Clinton) retained lifetime protection under the original 1965 law.
The northernmost land action of the American Civil War was the St. Albans Raid, which occurred in St. Albans, Vermont, on October 19, 1864. It was a Confederate raid launched from Canada.
The surname Rudolph originates from Germanic roots, combining "Rod" or "Hrōð" meaning "fame" and "olf" meaning "wolf" (effectively meaning "fame-wolf").
The flag of the Netherlands is a horizontal tricolor consisting of the colors red, white, and blue.

For sudden chest pain, patients should take aspirin (if advised by EMS and they have no history of recent serious bleeding) as it increases survival in acute coronary syndrome. EMS may also use supplemental oxygen if oxygen levels are low or there is respiratory distress, and sometimes Entonox, though its effectiveness lacks strong evidence.

The majority of phosphorus in the environment is stored in rocks and minerals on land. These deposits often originated in the ocean or from guano and were brought to land by geologic processes. While weathering releases soluble phosphorus for use by plants and animals, and it cycles through ecosystems, the primary long-term reservoir is terrestrial geological formations.
The Mexican Seismic Alert System is the name of the first earthquake early warning system; it began operations in 1991.

"Robin Hood: Prince of Thieves" was primarily filmed on location in the United Kingdom. Key English locations included Burnham Beeches (outlaw camp), Aysgarth Falls (Robin vs. Little John fight), Hardraw Force (Marian sees Robin bathing), Sycamore Gap (confronting sheriff's men), Seven Sisters cliffs (Robin's return), Wardour Castle (Locksley Castle), and Hulne Priory (Marian's manor). However, a second unit also filmed the medieval walls of Carcassonne, France to portray Nottingham and its castle.

The president gets authority to issue executive orders from powers granted in Article II of the U.S. Constitution or from specific authority delegated to the executive branch by Congress through statutes.
Lori Rom was originally cast as the youngest sister Phoebe Halliwell in the unaired pilot episode of Charmed, but she quit and was replaced by Alyssa Milano when a new pilot was filmed.

The US dollar effectively left the gold standard in October 1973 when it was allowed to float freely after a devaluation to $42.22 per ounce proved insufficient. The formal legal break, removing all references to gold from the dollar's definition, occurred in October 1976.

Jenna Boyd played the character Bailey Graffman, a leukemia patient, in the 2005 film *The Sisterhood of the Traveling Pants*. Her career includes earlier roles in *The Missing* and *Dickie Roberts: Former Child Star*, and later roles in *The Gathering*, *Ghost Whisperer*, and *Atypical*.

Who plays white is decided by drawing lots for the first game of a match, with colors alternating thereafter. In round-robin tournaments, the schedule is structured so players get roughly equal whites and blacks (exactly equal with odd players, one extra white/black with even players). Directors adjust schedules if players withdraw to prevent large color imbalances. The double-round-robin format ensures each player has the same number of white and black games against every opponent.

The word "bayonet" entered the English language from French in the late 16th century, derived from "Bayonne" (a city in France), where a type of long knife called a "bayonette" was made. Early English descriptions (like Cotgrave's 1611 dictionary) defined it simply as a kind of knife or dagger, and it's unclear if the earliest references meant a firearm attachment or just a knife.

The Han Dynasty achieved significant advancements across multiple fields. They revolutionized writing with the invention of papermaking, creating a cheap and practical writing medium. Transportation was improved by inventions like the wheelbarrow, the maritime junk ship, and the stern-mounted steering rudder, enabling sea travel. Navigation was enhanced through grid-reference maps and raised-relief maps. In medicine, they utilized herbal remedies, calisthenics, and regulated diets. They invented the seismometer to detect earthquakes. Their lunisolar calendar marked seasons and events, supported by progress in astronomy and mathematics, including discoveries like square/cube roots, the Pythagorean theorem, Gaussian elimination, the Horner scheme, improved pi calculations, and negative numbers. Extensive infrastructure development included building hundreds of roads and canals, as well as various bridge types (beam, arch, suspension, pontoon). Durable defensive city walls were also constructed.

Knowledge bases provide structured data represented as an object model or ontology, consisting of classes, subclasses, and instances interconnected by pointers, rather than simple tables of numbers and strings.

An Open University Grade 3 pass is awarded for module scores between 55% and 69%. This final grade is calculated as the *lower* of the student's overall continuous assessment score (OCAS) and overall examination score (OES).

Regina's mother, Adriana Vasquez, moved out of the Kennishes' guest house (where she lived with Regina and Daphne) after she found out Regina had married her ex-husband Angelo Sorrento. She later moved back in after she and Regina reconciled and patched things up.

Jenna is revealed to have her sight (or to have been faking blindness) in season 3, episode 1, titled "Blood is the New Black." This occurs when the Liars, suspecting she can see after Toby finds recent prescription eye drops, plant an earring in a school bathroom. Jenna picks up and examines the earring, proving she is not blind.

While the three-point line was experimentally tested in a 1945 NCAA game, it was not adopted then. The first league to officially start using the three-pointer was the American Basketball League (ABL) in 1961. The Eastern Professional Basketball League followed, implementing it in the 1963-64 season.
During the day, green tree frogs sleep in cool, dark, and moist hiding places such as tree holes or rock crevices. They choose these spots to conserve moisture and avoid drying out.
The water in the California Aqueduct comes from the Sierra Nevada Mountains and the valleys of Northern and Central California.

The Canadian Shield's main industry is mining, as it is exceptionally rich in mineral ores like nickel, gold, silver, and copper, supporting numerous mining towns, with Sudbury, Ontario being the largest and most well-known example.

The US government forms a more perfect Union primarily by establishing a federal government under the Constitution that acts directly on citizens (e.g., through taxation) and possesses significantly more power than the previous government under the Articles of Confederation, which could only act indirectly through the states. This creates a government over the people, not merely an agreement between states. Furthermore, the Constitution explicitly does not allow for state nullification of federal laws, dissolution of the Union, or secession, thereby strengthening and perfecting the Union.
A mini bike is defined by being a miniature motorcycle, typically featuring two-stroke engines and chain drive. Its small size is the primary characteristic that makes it "mini."

The mandatory service period for men in the Israeli military is 36 months, and for women, it is 24 months. This requirement applies to citizens and permanent residents at age 18, although exemptions exist for various reasons. These durations resulted from a 1968 amendment adding 6 months to the original periods of 30 months for men and 18 months for women.

The Chief Justice of India is usually appointed from the senior-most judge of the Supreme Court (by date of appointment), as recommended by the outgoing Chief Justice and appointed by the President of India.

Bob Dylan wrote the song "Make You Feel My Love" for his 1997 album *Time Out of Mind*. Although Billy Joel released the first commercial version titled "To Make You Feel My Love" earlier that year, Dylan is the original songwriter.
The name Marvin originates from the Welsh name Mervyn.

Immigrants entered the US on the west coast primarily through the Angel Island Immigration Station in San Francisco Bay. This facility operated from 1910 to 1940, detaining and processing hundreds of thousands of immigrants, the majority from China, Japan, India, Mexico, and the Philippines. It served as the main point of entry and processing for immigrants arriving on the west coast during that period.
San Miguel de Allende is a city in the far eastern part of the state of Guanajuato, in central Mexico. It is part of the Bajío macroregion.
Sabrina the Teenage Witch ran for seven seasons, totaling 163 episodes.

Staind is an American rock band formed in 1995. The lead singer's name is Aaron Lewis, who is also the rhythm guitarist. He has been the consistent lead vocalist since the band's original lineup, which included Mike Mushok (lead guitar), Johnny April (bass), and Jon Wysocki (drums, replaced in 2011). The band has released seven studio albums, sold over 15 million records, and achieved significant chart success with singles like "It's Been Awhile". While active sporadically after 2011, with Lewis pursuing a solo country career, the band entered a hiatus as of 2018.

The MacBook Pro 13-inch was introduced on June 8, 2009, at Apple's Worldwide Developers Conference (WWDC). It was created by upgrading and rebranding the existing 13-inch unibody MacBook as part of the MacBook Pro line. Key features at launch included a non-user-removable battery with up to 7 hours claimed life, the NVIDIA GeForce 9400M GPU, a screen with a 60% greater color gamut, a FireWire 800 port, an SD card slot, and the Kensington lock slot moved to the right side.

Electrical impulses that stimulate the heart muscle to contract are generated in the sinoatrial node. This signal then travels through the atrioventricular node, Bundle of His, and bundle branches, causing the atria and then the ventricles to contract and pump blood.
The Left Behind series is a 16-book religious novel series about the Christian biblical apocalypse and End Times, depicting a global conflict between converts and the Antichrist's world government.
Hank Williams sang "My Bucket's Got a Hole in It" for MGM in 1949, reaching #4 on the country chart.

Lymph is initially identical in composition to interstitial fluid when it first forms from it. However, the main difference is that as lymph flows through lymph nodes, it accumulates cells (particularly lymphocytes) and proteins, changing its composition compared to interstitial fluid.
U.S. President John F. Kennedy spoke the words "Ich bin ein Berliner" during his speech in West Berlin on June 26, 1963.

Creatine phosphate is found inside cells, primarily in muscle cells (which contain 95% of the body's creatine), and to a lesser extent in the brain, heart, and pancreas. It is formed from non-phosphorylated creatine by the enzyme creatine kinase once the creatine enters these cells.
The Chicago Cubs won the World Series in 1907, 1908, and 2016.

India's first Arctic research station, named Himadri Station, was set up during its second Arctic expedition in June 2008 and inaugurated on July 1, 2008. It is located at Ny-Ålesund, Svalbard, Norway.
Cilia are organelles found in eukaryotic cells (which include animal cells) and are located as slender protuberances projecting from the much larger cell body.
Lana and Clark get back together and begin a real relationship in the season seven episode "Fierce", after Clark's secret is no longer an issue.
Leonardo da Vinci painted the world-famous painting The Last Supper in the late 15th century.
The Atlantic Ocean has an elongated, S-shaped basin.

Abbot Suger is credited with creating the Gothic art movement, as it emerged with the construction of the Abbey Church of St Denis in Île-de-France, France, under his direction in the early 12th century. The style then spread rapidly to other art forms and across Europe.
The common currency of the European Union is the euro, which is used by the majority of its member states and its institutions.

The New Mexico whiptail lizard is an all-female species that reproduces asexually through parthenogenesis, where they produce eggs without fertilization. Despite this asexual reproduction, they engage in mating behavior with other females, which stimulates ovulation.
Ric Flair has won the most United States Championships overall, with six reigns. John Cena holds the record for the most reigns specifically under the WWE banner, with five.

The Confederate States Army (C.S.A.) was the official military land force of the Confederate States of America during the American Civil War (1861-1865), including at the Battle of Gettysburg. It was established by the Provisional Confederate Congress in early 1861, with Jefferson Davis as its commander-in-chief.

Merle Dixon is Daryl Dixon's older brother in The Walking Dead. He is portrayed as a misogynistic and racist Southern redneck hunter. Initially part of the survivors' group, he disappears after an encounter with Rick Grimes and later becomes the right-hand man to The Governor in Woodbury, creating conflict with Rick's group.
The Brazos River starts at the head of Blackwater Draw in Curry County, New Mexico, and stops (ends) at the Gulf of Mexico.

Malware is the umbrella term for malicious software intentionally designed to act against the user's requirements, including programs that damage, disable, or steal data. This encompasses viruses, worms, ransomware, spyware, and other harmful forms.
Ann Gillespie played Jackie, the mother of Kelly Taylor on *90210*.
Alistair in Dragon Age: Origins is voiced by Steve Valentine.

Don Shula has the most wins ever by an NFL head coach, retiring after the 1995 season with 328 regular season wins and 19 playoff wins, surpassing the previous record held by George Halas (324 wins including playoffs).
Lionel Richie's hit single "All Night Long (All Night)" was released in 1983 as part of his second solo album, *Can't Slow Down*.
Howard Caine played Gestapo Major Wolfgang Hochstetter in the television series Hogan's Heroes.

We use facework (protective self-presentation strategies like subtractive and repudiative tactics) to manage others' impressions when our desired online image is threatened. This occurs in response to undesirable photos (prompting un-tagging or removal requests), negative comments (requiring defensive posts), or negative outside evaluations concerning our physical appearance, competence, or approval. The motivation is to maintain self-esteem and avoid social rejection or criticism.

The song "Diamonds and Rust" is about Joan Baez's relationship with Bob Dylan, as she explicitly stated. It recounts a phone call from him that triggered memories of their time together in Greenwich Village around 1964/1965.

The phrase "I disapprove of what you say, but I will defend to the death your right to say it" was written by Evelyn Beatrice Hall in her book *The Friends of Voltaire* to illustrate Voltaire's beliefs about free speech. Although often mistakenly attributed to Voltaire himself, Hall's quotation clearly captures the principle of defending someone's right to express an opinion, even if you disagree with it, and is frequently cited in discussions of freedom of speech.

The passage distinguishes between power (the ability to influence actions) and authority (a legitimate claim, justification, and official right to exercise that power). The term used to identify having the official power to act is authority, as illustrated by a court's legal right to punish criminals versus a mob's mere power.

The excretory system helps maintain homeostasis by removing excess, unnecessary materials and waste products of metabolism from the body fluids. This elimination (primarily as urine, but also through exhalation and sweating in mammals) prevents damage and maintains internal chemical balance.

The name "Blue Peter" was chosen by Reed after he was inspired by seeing the "Blue Peter" maritime signal flag on a ship during a visit to Cheshire. He specifically selected this name because the flag signifies a vessel is about to leave, representing the 'voyage of adventure' he intended the programme to embark upon. An additional point noted was that blue is a popular children's colour and Peter is a common friend's name.
EDF Energy handles 5.7 million customer accounts in the UK.
The highest peak in the Ozarks is Buffalo Lookout, located in the Boston Mountains of Arkansas.

Japan has a constitutional monarchy system of government where the Emperor holds a primarily ceremonial role, and governmental power is divided among three branches: the Executive, the Legislative, and the Judicial.

The official codename for the US atomic bomb program was "Development of Substitute Materials," but this was superseded by the name "Manhattan Project," derived from the Army component's designation, the "Manhattan District."
There are two Death Stars in the Star Wars franchise: the first one and a second, larger, more advanced version.

Crowne Plaza is owned by InterContinental Hotels Group (IHG), as it forms part of their family of brands. It is an upscale, full-service hotel chain catering to business travelers, meetings, and conventions, with over 400 locations in 52 countries, typically found in city centers, resorts, coastal towns, or near major airports.

The Isle of Man is not part of the United Kingdom under British law, although the UK is responsible for its external and defence affairs. The passage details the historical absence of independent Manx military forces, listing various British-associated units raised in the past and noting a current British Army Reserve unit established in 2015.

Procol Harum's "A Whiter Shade of Pale" is described as an original adaptation of Bach's "Ich steh mit einem Fuß im Grabe" (BWV 156). Its iconic Hammond organ line is specifically derived from Bach's "Sleepers, Wake!" and "Air on the G String", sharing their characteristic stepwise bass motion. A closer, but less frequently cited, melodic influence comes from Bach's organ choral prelude "O Mensch bewein dein' Sünde groß" (BWV 622). The song also incorporates ideas from Percy Sledge's "When a Man Loves a Woman". The Bach similarities have been referenced in works like Tom Stoppard's *The Real Thing* and *The Commitments*.

The most documented origin suggests the Peaks of Otter were named by early European settlers, brothers Charles and Robert Ewing, after their Scottish Gaelic surname "Eóghain na h-Oitrich" (Clan Ewing of Otter), supported by similar Scottish place names and terrain resemblance. Alternative theories propose the name derives from the anglicized Cherokee word "Otari" (meaning "high places") or from the nearby Otter River, which was likely named for otters living downstream.
Season 8 of The Vampire Diaries premiered on October 21, 2016.
In the Only Fools and Horses Christmas special "Heroes and Villains", Del and Rodney attend a fancy dress party dressed as Batman and Robin.
Ray Charles wrote and released "Hallelujah I Love Her So" in 1956.

The Roman name for liberal arts education was **"liberalia studia"** or **"artes liberales"** (liberal pursuits/arts). Rooted in the Greek concept of "enkuklios paideia," this term was used during the Roman Empire, with Cicero being the first recorded user of "artes liberales." While the specific classification varied in Roman times, the canonical seven liberal arts were later solidified by Martianus Capella in the 5th century AD.
The first Horrid Henry book, titled "Horrid Henry", was written by Francesca Simon and published in 1994.

The Dallol crater is part of a cinder cone volcano located in the Danakil Depression within Ethiopia's Afar Region, northeast of the Erta Ale Range. It formed through the intrusion of basaltic magma into salt deposits and subsequent hydrothermal activity. A significant phreatic eruption in 1926 created Dallol Volcano itself, and numerous other eruption craters exist nearby on the salt flats. These craters are the lowest known subaerial volcanic vents globally, lying at least 45 meters below sea level. Additional activity includes a magma intrusion event in 2004 and another phreatic eruption in 2011.

The Spanish Civil War (1936-1939) was fought between the Republicans (loyal to the democratic Second Spanish Republic and allied with anarchists and communists) and the Nationalists (a conservative group led by General Francisco Franco, including Falangists, Carlists, and Catholics).
The Suez Canal is located in Egypt, connecting the Mediterranean Sea at Port Said to the Red Sea at Port Tewfik (Suez city), traversing the Isthmus of Suez.
The Comptroller (who also serves as the Auditor General and head of the National Audit Office) controls the Consolidated Fund.
The Sri Lanka Podujana Peramuna (SLPP), led by former president Mahinda Rajapaksa, won the 2018 election by securing the most seats and local authorities.
Frankie Valli sings "Can't Take My Eyes Off You". It was his major solo hit in 1967, reaching No. 2 on the Billboard Hot 100.
Lake Placid has hosted the Winter Olympics twice, specifically in 1932 and 1980, making it one of only three sites to have hosted the Winter Games on two occasions.

The term "hot rod" first appeared in the late 1930s in southern California, where it originated from the activity of racing modified cars on the dry lake beds northeast of Los Angeles under groups like the Southern California Timing Association (SCTA).
India won the ICC Under-19 World Cup 2018 by defeating Australia by 8 wickets in the final. This was their fourth title, the most by any team.
The Treaty of Paris formally acknowledged the United States as free, sovereign, and independent states, and this recognition (specifically Article 1 of the treaty) remains in force today.
Harry Potter's aunt and uncle, the Dursleys, live at Number 4, Privet Drive, Little Whinging, Surrey, England.
Carolyn Jones played Morticia Addams in the original black and white television series *The Addams Family*.
Pac-Man was released in the United States in October 1980. It was licensed for distribution there by Midway Games.
The first component of the International Space Station launched into orbit in 1998.

T-Bone Walker, an American blues electric guitar musician, originally sang and recorded "Mean Old World" in 1942. This recording is considered one of the first important blues recordings featuring the electric guitar.

The four nitrogenous bases used in DNA are adenine (A), thymine (T), guanine (G), and cytosine (C). Adenine always pairs with thymine, and guanine always pairs with cytosine. Uracil (U) is also a nitrogenous base, but it replaces thymine in RNA, meaning thymine is specific to DNA and uracil is specific to RNA. Structurally, thymine, cytosine, and uracil are pyrimidines (single ring), while adenine and guanine are purines (double ring).
The primary filming location for *Ghosts of Girlfriends Past* was Castle Hill in Ipswich, Massachusetts.
President James Madison chartered the Second Bank of the United States in 1816.

In *Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone*, Ian Hart (who also played Professor Quirrell) provided the voice and the facial source for Voldemort's manifestation on the back of Quirrell's head, which was heavily altered by CGI. Additionally, Richard Bremmer physically portrayed Voldemort in the flashback scene arriving at the Potter house, though his face is not seen.

The Battles of Lexington and Concord occurred in Middlesex County, Province of Massachusetts Bay, specifically within the towns of Lexington, Concord, Lincoln, Menotomy (present-day Arlington), and Cambridge.

The Pittsburgh Steelers have won the most Super Bowls in NFL history with six championships. The New England Patriots, Dallas Cowboys, and San Francisco 49ers are tied for the second-most wins with five Super Bowls each.

Most of Indiana observes Eastern Time, with only twelve counties (six near Chicago in the northwest and six near Evansville in the southwest) observing Central Time. Therefore, the majority of the state, including the central and eastern parts, is in the Eastern Time Zone.
The Final Four is being held at the Alamodome in San Antonio this year, marking the fourth time the city and venue have hosted the event.

Symbolic interactionism is a broad theoretical framework, not a specific testable theory. It serves as the basis for specific theoretical models like role theory and Identity Theory (Stryker's version and Burke's version). These derived theories use the interactionist framework to clearly define concepts and relationships within specific contexts, allowing them to be tested, unlike the broader framework itself. The framework is often criticized for being too vague for direct testing or empirical analysis, but it provides the foundation for these more concrete models.

The French Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen was primarily drafted by the Marquis de Lafayette (who prepared key drafts, sometimes consulting Thomas Jefferson) and Honoré Mirabeau (who played a central role in conceptualizing and drafting it in August 1789), based on Enlightenment ideals.
The song "...Baby One More Time" was written by Max Martin and performed by Britney Spears. It was released in 1998 as her debut single.

The training camp for the Titans football team depicted in "Remember the Titans" took place at Gettysburg College. It was during this camp that the players initially clashed racially but ultimately achieved unity through Coach Boone's demanding coaching, including a pivotal run to the Gettysburg cemetery and motivational speech.
Lauren Tom voices the character Amy Wong on the animated TV series Futurama.
The song "Push It" by Salt-N-Pepa was first released in 1987 as the B-side to the "Tramp" single and then released as its own single in 1988.

The song "Show Me the Meaning of Being Lonely" explores the feeling of loneliness stemming from heartbreak. The protagonist expresses confusion and pain over separation, questioning the nature of loneliness ("Is this the feeling I need to walk with?") and yearning to be with the absent person ("Tell me why I can't be there where you are"). The core meaning conveyed is an emptiness and profound sense of loss ("something missing in my heart") caused by the absence of a loved one.
The Nobel Prize in Literature was first awarded in 1901.
The first underground line of the New York City subway opened on October 27, 1904.

The Mexico earthquake occurred at a subduction zone boundary, specifically where the Cocos Plate is subducting beneath the North American Plate along the Pacific Coast of Mexico. This plate boundary is the primary source of large seismic events in the region.
One standard beer in the US (12 fl oz or 350 ml) contains about 14 grams of alcohol.

The US Department of Homeland Security was created on November 25, 2002, by the Homeland Security Act of 2002, to consolidate various U.S. executive branch organizations related to homeland security into a single Cabinet agency.

"By The Devil (I Was Tempted)" was a single written by Guy Fletcher and Doug Flett, released by Blue Mink as the associated single from their fourth album *Only When I Laugh*. It reached No. 26 on the charts.

Pacta sunt servanda requires states to perform treaty obligations in good faith. Crucially, this principle means a state party to a treaty cannot use its own municipal (domestic) law as a justification for failing to fulfill the treaty obligations, favoring the treaty over conflicting domestic law. While pacta sunt servanda is related to good faith, the passage clarifies they are not identical and cautions against equating them solely based on sources like the Vienna Convention or UNIDROIT Principles, which are influenced by civil law.
Julio Iglesias' 1976 live recording of "La Mer" plays during the last three and a half minutes of *Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy*, ending simultaneously with the film.
