Ole Einar Bjørndalen of Norway won every men's biathlon event at the 2002 Winter Olympics. He claimed gold in all three individual events and was part of Norway's gold-medal winning relay team.
Brent Barry won the NBA Slam Dunk Contest in 1996. He is a retired American NBA player, son of Rick Barry, who won two championships with the San Antonio Spurs.
The first orca put in captivity was a female named Wanda, captured in November 1961 for Marineland of the Pacific in Los Angeles. She died the day after being placed in a tank.
The first day collection of the Assamese film Mission China was ₹ 39.97 lakh.

Jyoti Basu, a leader of the Communist Party of India (Marxist), served as the Chief Minister of West Bengal from 1977 to 2000. This tenure of 23 years made him the longest-serving Chief Minister in India's history, a record he still held as of January 2015.

The most runs ever scored in a single MLB inning is 18, achieved by the Chicago White Stockings (now Cubs) against the Detroit Wolverines on September 6, 1883. The modern-day record (post-1900) is 17 runs, set by the Boston Red Sox against the Detroit Tigers on June 18, 1953.

The idea of castles was brought to England by the Norman invaders led by William the Conqueror, who established them starting in 1066 for defensive purposes, to pacify the inhabitants, and as bases for operations as he advanced through the country.

The passage describes events leading to the First Battle of El Alamein. Generalfeldmarschall Erwin Rommel's Panzer Army Africa advanced deep into Egypt by July 1942, threatening the Suez Canal and Palestine. General Auchinleck withdrew the British Eighth Army to a defensive position near the coast at El Alamein, Egypt, utilizing the Qattara Depression for protection. The Axis advance was halted at this location in early July during the First Battle of El Alamein. Therefore, both battles of El Alamein took place in Egypt.

The practice of saying "bless you" after a sneeze originated in ancient times (at least AD 77) from superstitious beliefs that sneezing expelled the soul, made the body vulnerable to evil spirits or the Devil, was an attempt to expel evil, or caused the heart to stop. The phrase "God bless you" or "bless you" was used as a protective charm against these perceived dangers.
Baga Beach is in North Goa.

The first case of what became known as Alzheimer's disease was diagnosed in 1901 by German psychiatrist Alois Alzheimer. He identified the condition in a fifty-year-old woman named Auguste D. He followed her case until her death in 1906 and then publicly reported on it.

The Mercedes-Benz G-Wagon (G-Class) is a mid-size four-wheel drive luxury SUV characterized by its boxy styling and body-on-frame construction. It is manufactured by Magna Steyr and sold by Mercedes-Benz.
Juice Newton sings the version of "Angel of the Morning" featured in the opening titles of Deadpool.

The costs for repairs on "Holmes Makes It Right" are covered by several parties: the homeowners pay a small portion (10-20%), some contractors donate time, materials, and labor, the TV production company funds the majority of the remaining work, and Mike Holmes sometimes contributes his own personal funds.

After WWI began in 1914, the United States declared neutrality under President Wilson. It attempted to broker peace while insisting on its neutral rights to trade and loan to belligerents. However, the British blockade and German actions, particularly unrestricted submarine warfare (highlighted by the sinking of the Lusitania in 1915 killing Americans) and atrocities, turned American public opinion against Germany. Despite German apologies and temporary pauses, Germany resumed unrestricted submarine warfare in early 1917 and sent the Zimmermann Telegram to Mexico. These acts, combined with the sinking of American merchant ships, led Wilson to abandon neutrality. He asked for and obtained a declaration of war against Germany in April 1917, framing it as a war to end militarism and all wars. The US then provided significant military support to the Allies, contributing to Germany's defeat. Wilson also promoted his vision ("Wilsonianism") through the Fourteen Points, shaping the diplomatic and moral narrative.
World War II ended in the Pacific on 14 and 15 August 1945 with the surrender of the armed forces of the Empire of Japan to the Allies.

A caterpillar hatches on Sunday and spends the week eating increasing amounts of fruit (one apple Monday, two pears Tuesday, etc.). On Saturday, he overeats a large variety of human foods, gets a stomach ache, and feels sick. After recovering on Sunday by eating a green leaf, he spins a cocoon. Two weeks later, he emerges transformed into a beautiful butterfly.

The current legal age for marriage in Australia is 18 years for both males and females. This uniform age was established by the Sex Discrimination Amendment Act 1991, which raised the marriageable age for females from the original 16 (set in 1961) to match the age of 18 that had always applied to males.
Jonathan Harris played the role of Dr. Zachary Smith in the 1960s science fiction television series *Lost in Space*.

Endotherms primarily generate their own heat internally through metabolic processes (e.g., increased metabolism when cold, aided by more mitochondria) to maintain a constant body temperature largely independent of the environment. In contrast, ectotherms rely almost entirely on external environmental sources and behavioral adaptations (like seeking sun or shade) to regulate their body temperature, making their internal temperature fluctuate with the surroundings. So, the key difference is the source of temperature regulation: internal metabolic heat production in endotherms versus external environmental sources and behavior in ectotherms.

The live-action filming for the new Pete's Dragon took place in New Zealand, specifically in Bay of Plenty, Taupo, Wellington, Rotorua, and Tapanui (where the Blue Mountain Lumber mill and main street were used). CGI work was done at Stone Street Studios.

The passage confirms the title is "Marley & Me", a 2008 American comedy-drama film based on John Grogan's memoir. Directed by David Frankel and starring Owen Wilson and Jennifer Aniston, it tells the story of a dog named Marley and his owners. The film was a box office success upon its Christmas 2008 release and was followed by a 2011 prequel.

The Great Victoria Desert sustains various lizards (including the great desert skink), small marsupials (like the sandhill dunnart and crest-tailed mulgara), burrowing animals (such as the southern marsupial mole and water-holding frog), birds (including the chestnut-breasted whiteface and malleefowl), and predators (like dingoes, perentie, and sand goanna monitor lizards). There are few large birds or mammals.
Robert Duvall portrayed the character Tom Hagen in the films *The Godfather* and *The Godfather Part II*.

Alkali metals are located in Group 1 of the periodic table, according to modern IUPAC nomenclature. This group includes lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, caesium, and francium. Hydrogen, although also placed in Group 1, is specifically excluded from the alkali metals because its chemical behavior is significantly different. Alkali metals are highly reactive, shiny, soft metals that readily form +1 cations, react vigorously with water and air (requiring storage under oil), and are never found free in nature.

IBM Lotus Notes provides business collaboration tools including email, calendars, to-do lists, contacts management, teamrooms, discussion forums, file sharing, microblogging, instant messaging, blogs, and user directories. It also integrates with other IBM Domino applications and databases. (Note: Integration with the IBM Lotus Symphony office suite was removed in version 9).

The Rough Riders were the nickname for the 1st United States Volunteer Cavalry regiment, raised in 1898 for the Spanish-American War due to the small size of the regular US Army. They were the only one of three volunteer cavalry regiments to see combat action in that war. Despite being a cavalry unit, they primarily fought on foot as infantry, leading to their alternative nickname "Wood's Weary Walkers" after their first commander, Colonel Leonard Wood.
Colby cheese is named after the village of Colby, Wisconsin, where Joseph F. Steinwand developed it in 1885 near his father's cheese factory.
Oona Chaplin plays Talisa Maegyr in the HBO TV series Game of Thrones.

The UK national debt is primarily owed to a wide range of investors. The largest domestic holders are pension funds, which hold government debt (like Treasury bonds) as assets. Additionally, a significant portion (27.6% as of end-2016) is owed to overseas governments and investors.
Route 66 originally started on the west coast at the southwest end of Santa Monica Boulevard in Santa Monica, California.

Lori dies in the episode "Killer Within". Her death, particularly the heartbreaking goodbye scene with her son Carl, was met with favorable reviews from television commentators who found it emotionally devastating, gut-churning, and a grim departure for the character. T-Dog also dies in the same episode.
Sara Gilbert is the actress who played Darlene Conner on the ABC sitcom *Roseanne* from 1988 to 1997, receiving two Emmy nominations for the role. She reprised the character for the 2018 revival.
The joint connecting the incus and malleus is the incudomalleolar (or incudomallear) joint, which is a synovial joint.

Your Airtel mobile number serves as your Airtel Payments Bank account number. Key features include earning 7.25% interest, getting equivalent talk time on initial deposits, free personal accident insurance, cash withdrawals at Airtel Banking Points (0.65% fee), transfers to other banks (0.5% fee, waived for other Airtel Payments Bank accounts), a virtual debit card, and a maximum deposit limit of ₹1 lakh per account. The bank leverages Airtel's vast network of outlets across India.
Tracy Lawrence sings "Find Out Who Your Friends Are" with guest vocalists Tim McGraw and Kenny Chesney on a remixed version featured on his album *For the Love*.
The original "Spirit in the Sky" was written and recorded by Norman Greenbaum. It was released in late 1969.

Vicente Fox of the Alliance for Change was elected president of Mexico in 2000, receiving 43.4% of the vote. This victory marked the first time the opposition won a presidential election since the Mexican Revolution.

Bhimrao Ramji Ambedkar, popularly known as Baba Saheb, was a jurist, social reformer, and Independent India's first law minister. He is recognized as the principal architect of the Constitution of India, earning him the title of the father of the Indian Constitution, and was also a founding father of the Republic of India.

The main judges on Dancing on Ice 2014 (The Ice Panel) were Robin Cousins (ninth series), Jason Gardiner (eighth series), Karen Barber (seventh series), and Ashley Roberts (second series). Robin Cousins was absent for weeks 6 and 7 due to the Winter Olympics; during his absence, former judge Nicky Slater filled in and Karen Barber served as temporary head judge.
Hero Beauregard Fiennes-Tiffin plays the 11-year-old Tom Riddle (young Voldemort) in *Harry Potter and the Half Blood Prince*, and he is the nephew of Ralph Fiennes who plays the adult Voldemort.

Greg Heffley is described as mischievous, lazy, paranoid, egotistical, dishonest, and easily jealous. He admits to being a poor friend, avoids blame, and constantly manipulates situations to climb his "popularity ladder," often becoming his own worst enemy. While he displays a kinder side occasionally, his schemes for popularity and money throughout the series consistently backfire.

Skyline Drive begins at its northern terminus at an intersection with US Route 340 near Front Royal, Virginia. It ends at its southern terminus at an interchange with US Route 250 near Interstate 64 in Rockfish Gap, Virginia.
The military plane crash in Mississippi occurred on Monday, July 10, 2017, at approximately 4:00 PM. The aircraft was a US Marine Corps KC-130T Hercules, and the crash site was in Leflore County.

Aluminum wiring was used in North American residential construction (including Ontario) from the 1960s until the mid-1970s, when its use declined due to safety concerns and compatibility issues with electrical devices.

Beta decay includes a process called positron emission where a proton within an atomic nucleus is converted into a neutron. This occurs through the emission of a positron and a neutrino, effectively turning the proton into a neutron to help the atom achieve a more stable ratio of protons to neutrons. For this decay to happen, it must be energetically possible (positive Q value).

The Great Depression began in the United States following a major fall in stock prices starting around September 4, 1929, which culminated in the worldwide news event of the stock market crash on October 29, 1929 (Black Tuesday).
The US men's national soccer team holds regular training camps at the U.S. Soccer National Training Center located in Carson, California.
Brisket comes from the breast or lower chest of the cow. Specifically, it is cut from the superficial and deep pectoral muscles located in this lower chest/breast area.
To Kill a Mockingbird became popular immediately upon its publication in 1960, as it was an immediate success and won the Pulitzer Prize.
James Monroe Iglehart played the Genie in the original Broadway production of *Aladdin*, a role for which he won a Tony Award.
The Great Pyramid of Giza was the tallest man-made structure in the world for over 3,800 years, until the construction of Lincoln Cathedral in 1300.
The Pir Panjal Railway Tunnel, India's longest railway tunnel, is constructed through the Pir Panjal Range in Jammu and Kashmir, connecting Quazigund and Banihal.
"Bennie and the Jets" was released in 1973 on Elton John's album Goodbye Yellow Brick Road.
The American rules version of croquet, governed by the United States Croquet Association, is a six-hoop game and is the dominant version played in the United States and Canada.
Michael Douglas, Kathleen Turner, and Danny DeVito starred in the movie Romancing the Stone.
Amazon was founded by Jeff Bezos on July 5, 1994, and its headquarters is based in Seattle, Washington.

To meet the requirement of informed consent, suspects must be given a written explanation of a caution's implications before accepting it. As part of this process, introduced by a 2008 Home Office circular, the offender **must sign a specific form** that details the consequences of accepting the caution.

According to the Kashmir Pandit Sangharsh Samiti (KPSS), 399 Kashmiri Pandits were killed by insurgents between 1990 and 2011. Specifically, they estimate that 357 were killed in 1990 alone. The passage also mentions a broader estimate of about 650 Pandits killed in the valley over the last 20 years (relative to the time of the survey).
The twelfth generation Ford F-150, representing a body style change, was introduced for the 2009 model year as an update to the platform.

The song "Imagine" was co-produced by John Lennon and Yoko Ono with Phil Spector. Recording began in England in May 1971 and finished in New York City in July 1971. Released as a single in the US one month after the September album release, it peaked at number three on the Billboard Hot 100. Although not initially a UK single, a 1975 re-release reached number six there. The song has sold over 1.6 million copies in the UK and reached number one following Lennon's murder in December 1980. Lennon acknowledged Yoko Ono's role in inspiring the song's concept shortly before his death, and plans were underway in 2017 to grant her a co-writing credit. The song is memorialized in Central Park's Strawberry Fields mosaic.

First Nations are located south of the Arctic across Canada. There are currently 634 recognized First Nations governments or bands spread throughout the country, with roughly half located specifically in the provinces of Ontario and British Columbia.
The name Robinson is an English patronymic surname, originating in England. It literally means "son of Robin", where Robin is a diminutive form of the name Robert.
Keith Thibodeaux played the character Little Ricky on both the television sitcom "I Love Lucy" and "The Lucy-Desi Comedy Hour".

The German word "Schadenfreude" literally means 'harm-joy' and refers to the feeling of pleasure, joy, or self-satisfaction that comes from learning about or witnessing the troubles, failures, or humiliation of another person.
Wilt Chamberlain holds the NBA single-game scoring record, having scored 100 points in a game in 1962.
Taylor Swift's debut album, titled *Taylor Swift*, was released in 2006, following her signing with Big Machine Records in 2005.
The current world number one ranked men's tennis player is Rafael Nadal.
Caleb Calhoun is Dylan's biological father. He is also Norma's estranged older brother and Norman's uncle.

Ireland gained independence from England in 1922 when most of the island seceded to form the Irish Free State, following the Irish War of Independence and the Anglo-Irish Treaty. However, six northeastern counties (Northern Ireland) remained part of the United Kingdom.

The real story behind Five Nights at Freddy's is that creator Scott Cawthon was inspired by criticism of his earlier game *Chipper & Sons Lumber Co.*, where players found the beaver character unintentionally terrifying like a "scary animatronic animal". Although initially discouraged, Cawthon used this negative feedback to intentionally create a scarier game, leading directly to the development of Five Nights at Freddy's.

Pride and Prejudice, a romantic novel by Jane Austen, was first published in 1813. It follows Elizabeth Bennet's emotional growth as she learns to overcome hasty judgments and understand deeper values, set against a backdrop of Regency-era manners, education, marriage, and money.

US military planners, particularly the Joint Chiefs of Staff in April 1945, projected significant casualties for the invasion of Kyushu (Operation Olympic). Estimates were based on two scenarios using different casualty rates: 1. "Pacific Experience" Scenario: For a 766,700-man force over 90 days: 134,556 dead or missing and 514,072 total casualties. 2. "European Experience" Scenario: For the same force and duration: 28,981 dead or missing and 149,046 total casualties. These estimates increased as known Japanese strength grew and were limited to the first 90 days of combat, excluding later casualties and losses at sea from air attacks. Planners anticipated needing 100,000 replacements per month.

At the North Pole, you will see Polaris positioned almost directly overhead, nearly motionless in the sky. This is because Polaris lies very close to the extension of the Earth's rotational axis above the North Pole (the north celestial pole). Consequently, all other stars in the northern sky appear to rotate around it.

Bing Crosby and Rudy Vallee sang "Buddy, Can You Spare a Dime?" (also known as "Brother, Can You Spare a Dime?"). Their recordings, released just before Franklin D. Roosevelt's 1932 election, became the most well-known versions, with Bing Crosby's rendition becoming the best-selling record of the period. The song was written by E.Y. Harburg and Jay Gorney.

By 1914, the only African countries that remained independent were Ethiopia and Liberia. This followed a period of rapid European colonial expansion across the continent, which had seen European control grow from just 10% of Africa in the 1870s.

The poem "9 – 3" by Max Boyce refers to the famous rugby match between Llanelli and the New Zealand All Blacks on 31 October 1972. The line "The day the pubs ran dry" describes the massive celebrations in the town after Llanelli's unexpected 9-3 victory, during which the pubs sold out of all alcoholic drinks. Therefore, the day the pubs ran dry was 31 October 1972.
The song "Tell Me Something Good" was sung by Rufus and Chaka Khan. It was written by Stevie Wonder and released in 1974.
Ireland has won the Eurovision Song Contest the most times, with a record seven victories. This includes winning three times consecutively from 1992 to 1994, another record.

Reverse transcriptase enables retroviral infection by converting the virus's RNA genome into DNA. This viral DNA is then integrated into the host cell's genome and replicated along with it, allowing the virus to persist and multiply. Without reverse transcriptase, retroviruses like HIV could not incorporate their genetic material into the host cell and would fail to replicate.

World War II is generally said to have been started by Nazi Germany. It began with the invasion of Poland by Nazi Germany on 1 September 1939, which was followed by declarations of war on Germany by France and the United Kingdom. While Japan had been at war with China since 1937, the invasion of Poland marked the outbreak of the global conflict.

On December 24, 1929 (Christmas Eve), the West Wing of the White House was significantly damaged by an electrical fire. This occurred less than seven months after President Herbert Hoover had completed a major rebuild of the wing. Hoover subsequently rebuilt the West Wing again and added air-conditioning.

Dust Cave is archaeologically significant primarily because its stratigraphy preserves five distinct cultural components spanning from approximately 10,650 cal B.C. to 3600 cal B.C. (Quad/Beaver Lake/Dalton, Early Side-Notched, Kirk Stemmed, Eva/Morrow Mountain, and Benton). This sequence provides a detailed record of human habitation and cultural change over thousands of years, starting from when the cave became habitable around 10,600 B.P. after river sediments deposited between 17,000 and 15,000 B.P. were flushed out. The well-defined layers (Zones A-Y) clearly separate these cultural periods.

In the first episode of "Wonders of the Universe", Brian Cox explores the nature of time. He contrasts human-scale time cycles (days, months, years) with cosmic cycles (like our solar system's 250 million year orbit around the Milky Way) at Chankillo, Peru. At Perito Moreno Glacier, he introduces the Arrow of Time and irreversible change using the gamma-ray burst GRB 090423. In Kolmanskop, he discusses the thermodynamic arrow of time, entropy, and stellar evolution. The episode concludes over the Skeleton Coast, using the wreck of the Eduard Bohlen to illustrate the concept of the universe's inevitable heat death.

The last time an NBA backboard was broken during an official NBA game was in 1993 by Chris Morris of the New Jersey Nets. Following the 1992-93 season, where Shaquille O'Neal broke two goal supports (though not the glass itself), the NBA significantly strengthened the backboard supports and stability to prevent such occurrences, making breaks extremely rare in official NBA games since then. While Joey Dorsey (a former NBA player) broke a backboard glass in a 2012 preseason exhibition game in Italy, this was not an official NBA game. Other listed breaks occurred in NCAA games or non-NBA events.
Carol Ann Susi provided the voice of the unseen character Mrs. Wolowitz, Howard Wolowitz's mother, on *The Big Bang Theory*.

A plant cutting is a piece of stem, root, or leaf placed in a suitable medium like moist soil. Under suitable conditions, the cutting undergoes "striking": a stem cutting develops new roots, a root cutting develops new stems, and a leaf cutting can develop both new stems and roots. This process creates a completely new, independent plant genetically identical to the parent plant.

The Beast is not a single animal species but a chimera, a composite creature made from parts of several animals. He combines features including the head/horns of a buffalo, body/arms of a bear, eyebrows of a gorilla, jaws/teeth/mane of a lion, tusks of a wild boar, and legs/tail of a wolf. He also resembles mythical monsters like the Minotaur or a werewolf.
A touchdown in American football is worth six points.
New Mexico was admitted to the Union as the 47th state on January 6, 1912, after previously being the U.S. New Mexico Territory, which was established following the Mexican-American War.

The total eclipse's land coverage in the contiguous United States ended along the South Carolina coast at about 6:44 p.m. UTC (which is 2:44 p.m. EDT). Partial eclipse visibility in Hawaii ended earlier, by 5:25 p.m. UTC (7:25 a.m. HST).

The first imperial power fighting in Vietnam was France. Full-scale war broke out between the Việt Minh and France in 1946 (the First Indochina War). France attempted to maintain influence by establishing the State of Vietnam in 1949 under Bảo Đại within the French Union. The war concluded in 1954 when Việt Minh forces defeated the French at Dien Bien Phu, leading France to relinquish its colonies. The Geneva Conference then divided Vietnam into communist North Vietnam and US-supported South Vietnam.
Varsity Blues is a 1999 American comedy-drama film.

Johannes Gutenberg developed the first printing press system in Europe, specifically around 1439. His key innovations were the hand-molded metal printing matrices and the movable type-based printing press, which were previously unknown in Europe.

Wood thrushes migrate at night. This nocturnal behavior allows them to navigate using the stars and the Earth's magnetic field. Their fall migration period is typically from mid-August to mid-September.

One full rotation of Earth relative to the Sun is about 24 hours (a solar day), while one full rotation relative to the stars is 23 hours, 56 minutes, and 4 seconds (a sidereal day). Earth's rotation is very gradually slowing due to the Moon's tidal effects, making a modern day about 1.7 milliseconds longer than a century ago.

Clarke Griffin was imprisoned for treason on the Ark because she acted as an accomplice to her father, Jake Griffin, who discovered the Ark's critical oxygen shortage and planned to reveal it publicly against the Chancellor's orders.

Before Waste Management became the sponsor in 2010, the tournament was sponsored by the investment bank Friedman Billings Ramsey (FBR) from 2004 to 2009, during which time it was called the FBR Open. Prior to FBR's sponsorship, it was known as the Phoenix Open for most of its history.
Australia II won the America's Cup in 1983 by defeating the defender yacht Liberty in the match races, ending the New York Yacht Club's 132-year winning streak.

The Toronto Maple Leafs currently play their home hockey games at the Air Canada Centre (now known as Scotiabank Arena) in Toronto, having moved there in February 1999. They previously played at Maple Leaf Gardens (1931-1999) and the Mutual Street Arena (before 1931).

The FCPA applies to: 1. U.S. businesses (domestic concerns). 2. Foreign companies that issue securities traded in the U.S. 3. U.S. nationals, citizens, and residents (wherever located, under the *nationality principle*). 4. Foreign natural and legal persons while they are physically within the territory of the U.S. (under the *protective principle*). 5. Any person (including those listed above) who uses means or instrumentalities of U.S. interstate commerce (like mail, phone, banking) in connection with a corrupt payment (under the *territoriality principle*).
Live From the Red Carpet, E!'s pre-show for major award ceremonies covering celebrity arrivals and fashion, is hosted by Ryan Seacrest and Giuliana Rancic.
Bob Newhart played the role of Professor Proton (Arthur Jeffries) in a 2013 guest appearance on *The Big Bang Theory*, for which he won his first Primetime Emmy Award.
Season 8 of Blue Bloods premiered on CBS on September 29, 2017.

Mainland Greece is the southern part of the Balkan peninsula, meaning it is land with water on three sides. The Peloponnese is a smaller peninsula projecting from it, connected by the Isthmus of Corinth.
Middle-earth: Shadow of Mordor was released for Microsoft Windows, PlayStation 4, and Xbox One in September 2014, and for PlayStation 3 and Xbox 360 in November 2014.

The curated soundtrack album, *Black Panther: The Album* by Kendrick Lamar, was released on **February 9, 2018**, while the original score soundtrack by Ludwig Göransson was released on **February 16, 2018**.
The song "Fly Me to the Moon" (originally titled "In Other Words") was written by Bart Howard in 1954.

Taxonomy is the science of classifying biological organisms into hierarchical groups (taxa) based on shared characteristics. The Swedish botanist Carl Linnaeus is regarded as the father of taxonomy, as he developed one of the first formal systems, known as Linnaean taxonomy, along with binomial nomenclature for naming organisms.

The first general election in India after independence was held in 1951–52, electing the first Lok Sabha. This followed India's independence in August 1947 and the interim governance by the Constituent Assembly.

Werner Heisenberg and Dmitri Ivanenko proposed the initial concept that protons and neutrons compose the atomic nucleus shortly after the neutron's discovery. Heisenberg specifically developed a quantum mechanical approach for the nucleus using these particles and introduced the first theory of nuclear exchange forces binding them, though he initially incorrectly assumed the neutron was a proton-electron composite.
Season 4 of The Flash began airing on October 10, 2017.

Simple squamous epithelium is found in locations requiring rapid diffusion or filtration, specifically in the capillaries (blood vessels), alveoli (air sacs in the lungs), glomeruli (filtering units in the kidneys), and the outer layer of the skin. Its thin, flat structure makes it permeable and ideal for these functions.

The cross symbol (a simple representation) was associated with Christians as early as the 2nd century, with widespread use and deep association by the early 3rd century (e.g., Clement of Alexandria calling it "the Lord's sign," Tertullian referring to believers as "devotees of the Cross"). However, the crucifix – a cross bearing an image of Christ – is explicitly stated to not be known until the 6th century AD.
Roger Nichols and Paul Williams wrote the song "Rainy Days and Mondays".

Werner Ruchti, a German engineer, designed the traffic circle in Long Beach, CA. He was contracted in 1930 to design it based on European models, and construction was expedited to handle traffic for the 1932 Summer Olympics events held in the city.
The Pardoner's Tale is an extended exemplum (a story told to illustrate a moral point), specifically demonstrating the theme "Radix malorum est cupiditas" ("Greed is the root of [all] evils").

Brian Lara, Sachin Tendulkar, and Kumar Sangakkara share the record for scoring the fastest 10,000 runs in Test cricket, each achieving the feat in the least number of innings (195). Steve Waugh took significantly longer (244 innings).
Red Dead Redemption was released on May 18, 2010, after being delayed from its original projected April 2010 release date.
The first mandatory driver's licenses were required starting on 1 January 1904 in the United Kingdom, following the Motor Car Act 1903.
Prince William, Duke of Cambridge (the elder son of Charles, Prince of Wales) is next in line to inherit the British throne.

Flash drive write endurance depends on the memory type: multi-level cell (MLC) memory typically handles 3,000-5,000 program-erase cycles (writes), while single-level cell (SLC) memory can endure around 100,000 writes.

There have been 27 amendments ratified and added to the United States Constitution. While Congress has proposed 33 amendments in total, only 27 have received the necessary state ratifications to become part of the Constitution. The first ten ratified amendments are known as the Bill of Rights. Six proposed amendments failed to achieve ratification.
Tritium is the hydrogen isotope with the symbol 3H. Its nucleus contains one proton and two neutrons, giving it a mass number of 3.

The original cast of *Natasha, Pierre and the Great Comet of 1812* at its 2012 Ars Nova premiere included Dave Malloy as Pierre, Phillipa Soo as Natasha, Lucas Steele as Anatole, Amber Gray as Hélène, Brittain Ashford as Sonya, Nick Choksi as Dolokhov, Shaina Taub as Mary, Blake DeLong as Andrey/Prince Bolkonsky, Amelia Workman as Marya D., and Paul Pinto as Balaga.
Tamara Drasin sang "Smoke Gets in Your Eyes" first in the original 1933 Broadway musical *Roberta*. Gertrude Niesen recorded the song first on October 13, 1933.
The team that lost the pre-game coin toss kicks the ball first to start the football game.

Voting for the Baseball Hall of Fame is done by two groups: 1) Eligible members of the Baseball Writers' Association of America (BBWAA) (those with 10+ years membership actively covering MLB within the last 10 years) for recently retired players, and 2) The Veterans Committee (comprising four era-based subcommittees) for players no longer eligible on the BBWAA ballot and other baseball figures.

Hunter Tylo portrayed Taylor Hayes on *The Bold and the Beautiful*, debuting in 1990. She appeared regularly until 1994, took breaks, left in 1996 but returned shortly after, was written out in 2002, returned as a ghost in 2004, and permanently returned (revealed alive) in 2005. She exited again in 2013 but made guest appearances in 2014 and returned once more in 2018.

Louis XV significantly expanded the Palace of Versailles, adding structures like the petit appartement du roi, appartements de Mesdames, appartement du dauphin, appartement de la dauphine, his own private apartments on the upper floors, the Opéra, and the Petit Trianon.

The NBA playoffs use a fixed bracket without reseeding. In the first round (conference quarterfinals), the matchups are strictly determined by seed: 1 vs 8, 2 vs 7, 3 vs 6, and 4 vs 5. The winners advance to subsequent rounds as outlined in the bracket.

Canada gained full independence from Great Britain with the signing of the Constitution Act, 1982 by Queen Elizabeth II on April 17, 1982, establishing Canada's complete sovereignty as an independent country. While the Queen remains the Head of State of Canada, her role is separate from her role as the British monarch.
Jacob Marley died seven years before the main events of *A Christmas Carol*, specifically on December 24, 1836 (Christmas Eve), as the story begins on Christmas Eve 1843.
Rockefeller Christmas trees come from nurseries in the tri-state area (NY, NJ, CT), unique backyard trees scouted by the Head Gardener, and trees submitted by the public via their website.
Originally a daytime event, the State of the Nation Address is now delivered in the evening, specifically after 9:00 PM Eastern Time (ET), to maximize viewership through live television broadcasts.
Wake Forest is located primarily in Wake County, North Carolina, just north of the state capital, Raleigh. A small portion also lies in Franklin County.

In parliamentary systems, the head of government is almost always a member of parliament and is answerable to it. This holds true whether the country is a constitutional monarchy (like the UK or Japan) or a parliamentary republic (like Germany or India). Even in the few parliamentary republics where the head of government is also the head of state (e.g., South Africa), they are elected by parliament. Typically, in bicameral systems, the head of government comes from the lower house.

The "Mini" in Minigun comes from its smaller size and caliber compared to larger rotary-barreled weapons like the 20mm M61 Vulcan, while "gun" indicates its use of rifle-caliber bullets instead of autocannon shells.
Hanna Alström played Crown Princess Tilde of Sweden.
The most well-known version of "The Three Little Pigs" is the Walt Disney-produced Silly Symphony cartoon, which came out in 1933.

Andrew Johnson was the first U.S. president to be impeached (in 1868), stemming from his conflict with the Republican Congress over Reconstruction policies after the Civil War, particularly his defiance of legislation they passed. Although impeached by the House, he was acquitted by the Senate. The passage suggests this impeachment was seen as politically motivated ("an act of political expedience, rather than necessity") and unpopular with the public, even though Johnson himself was unpopular.

Papayas on the Big Island of Hawaii (specifically in the Puna region) were suffering from **PRSV (Papaya Ringspot Virus)**. The virus, which had devastated production on Oahu decades earlier, was found in Big Island home gardens in 1971 and emerged in commercial farms in 1992, making production impossible in Puna by 1995.
The South Asian Association for Regional Co-operation (SAARC) was formed in Dhaka on 8 December 1985.
The song "Would I Lie to You?" was written by Mike Leeson and Peter Vale. It was performed by the duo Charles & Eddie.

The Lord of the Rings film series directed by Peter Jackson consists of three films: *The Fellowship of the Ring* (2001), *The Two Towers* (2002), and *The Return of the King* (2003), based on J.R.R. Tolkien's novel.
McVitie's successfully argued in a 1991 VAT tribunal that Jaffa Cakes should be classified as cakes, not biscuits, for tax purposes. This legal ruling definitively categorizes them as cakes.

Seth MacFarlane provides the voice for Stewie Griffin in Family Guy, along with the characters Peter Griffin and Brian Griffin. He chose to voice them himself to match his original vision, basing Stewie's voice specifically on English actor Rex Harrison.

Auditing is the process of verifying the accuracy and fairness of financial statements through an objective, independent examination, which provides reasonable (but not absolute) assurance that they are presented fairly according to the applicable financial reporting framework, thereby increasing their credibility and value.
Thad Luckinbill is the actor who plays J.T. Hellstrom on *The Young and the Restless*, portraying the character from August 1999 to November 2010 and again starting in December 2017.

The first commercial cell phone, the Motorola DynaTAC 8000X, was released on March 6, 1983, by Ameritech on the first US 1G network. It took over a decade and $100 million to develop. Despite limitations like short battery life and long charging times, consumer demand was very strong, resulting in waiting lists in the thousands.

Casey was named after an Irish non-player Thayer knew, but it's debated if the character was modeled after anyone specific; however, Thayer's best friend Samuel Winslow, a Harvard baseball player, has been suggested as the inspiration for the character.
The first film in the "Ice Age" animated franchise, produced by Blue Sky Studios, was released in 2002.

Toru Dutt's poem uses the Casuarina tree as a powerful symbol and medium connecting her present to cherished childhood memories. While describing the tree in detail, its true significance lies in evoking memories of playing beneath it with her deceased siblings, Abju and Aru. These memories provide her comfort abroad but also cause sorrow, highlighting the importance of memory. Her deep love for the tree stems from its association with her lost loved ones. Ultimately, she immortalizes the tree in poetry, hoping it will endure like Wordsworth's Borrowdale yews, ensuring the memory of her siblings lives on through it.
Ed Roland is the lead singer (lead vocalist) of the American rock band Collective Soul.

The President of the United States is elected indirectly through the Electoral College. Registered voters in the states and D.C. cast ballots for electors pledged to specific presidential candidates. These electors then formally vote for President and Vice President. A candidate must win an absolute majority of electoral votes (currently at least 270 out of 538) to be elected. If no presidential candidate achieves this majority, the House of Representatives chooses the President; if no vice-presidential candidate does, the Senate chooses the Vice President.
Dwyane Wade currently plays for the Miami Heat. After playing for the Chicago Bulls and Cleveland Cavaliers for 1½ seasons, he was traded back to Miami in February 2018.

In Western culture, particularly European folklore, the white stork is a symbol of delivering babies to new parents. The legend, popularized by Hans Christian Andersen, holds that storks find babies (in caves/marshes called "stork stones") and bring them to households, often dropping them down the chimney. People even placed sweets on windowsills to attract storks when they desired children. This symbolism is so strong that a birthmark on a newborn's neck is called a "stork-bite".

Honors in contract bridge can be declared at any time after the auction concludes, but it is strategically best to declare them at the conclusion of play to avoid giving opponents information about card distribution. Any player, including dummy, may hold honors.

The three phases of the normal blood clotting process (hemostasis) are: 1. Vascular Spasm: The immediate constriction of the damaged blood vessel to reduce blood flow and loss. 2. Platelet Plug Formation: Platelets adhere to the site of injury and stick together, forming a temporary seal over the break in the vessel wall. 3. Coagulation (Blood Clotting): Fibrin strands form and reinforce the platelet plug, creating a stable blood clot that acts as a "molecular glue" to stop the bleeding.

Valles Marineris formed primarily due to crustal fracturing caused by the immense weight of the Tharsis Bulge. As the Tharsis region grew through massive volcanism, it overloaded the Martian crust. During the second stage of Tharsis's formation, the crust failed to support this load, leading to the formation of radial fractures, including those that would become the Valles Marineris canyon system.

The decision to leave the EU was made by the UK electorate in a referendum held on 23 June 2016, where 51.9% voted to leave. The UK government formally triggered the withdrawal process by invoking Article 50 on 29 March 2017.

The principle of separation of powers and checks and balances was specifically credited to Montesquieu during the Enlightenment, particularly in his 1748 work *The Spirit of the Laws*. His ideas were implemented in the 1787 Constitution of the United States. Checks and balances ensure each branch of government (executive, legislative, judicial) can limit the others, preventing any one branch from becoming supreme and protecting the people from government abuses.

Responsibility for redistricting varies by state. In 28 states, the state legislature has primary responsibility, often subject to gubernatorial approval. To reduce political influence, 12 states (Alaska, Arizona, California, Colorado, Hawaii, Idaho, Missouri, Montana, New Jersey, Ohio, Pennsylvania, Washington) use independent or bipartisan commissions for congressional districts. Five states (Maine, New York, Rhode Island, Vermont, Virginia) have independent bodies propose plans, but legislatures must approve them. Arkansas uses a commission of the Governor, Attorney General, and Secretary of State. Seven low-population states (Alaska, Delaware, Montana, North Dakota, South Dakota, Vermont, Wyoming) have only one representative and do not need redistricting.
Idaho borders Montana to the west and southwest.

Martin Luther King Jr.'s "I Have a Dream" speech begins by noting the unfulfilled promise of freedom for Black Americans a century after the Emancipation Proclamation. Its most famous section, where King describes his dreams of freedom and equality rising from slavery and hatred, was a partly improvised departure from his prepared text, prompted by Mahalia Jackson's call to "Tell them about the dream." This iconic speech is widely regarded as one of the most significant in American history, ranked as the top American speech of the 20th century.

Blood alcohol concentration (BAC) refers to the mass of alcohol present in relation to either the volume of blood (mass per volume, e.g., grams per deciliter or grams per liter) or the mass of blood (mass per mass). It is never defined as a volume of alcohol per volume of blood.
In English, the euro sign (€) precedes the numerical value (e.g., €10, not 10 €), and some style guides recommend no space between the sign and the number.
"Rapper's Delight" by the Sugarhill Gang was released in 1979.

In England and Wales, owning a lockpick set is not automatically illegal. However, the law presumes that possessing items specifically made for burglary or theft, like lockpicks, indicates an intent to commit such crimes. This means you can be prosecuted for possession alone without the prosecution needing to prove your intent separately. The penalty can be up to 3 years imprisonment. Legitimate reasons for possession (like locksmithing) would need to be demonstrated to counter this presumption.
Deion Sanders played Major League Baseball (MLB) for the New York Yankees, the Atlanta Braves, the Cincinnati Reds, and the San Francisco Giants.

The film Titanic earned a total of $2.187 billion worldwide, comprising $659.4 million in North America and $1.528 billion in other countries, including revenue from its original release and reissues. It was the first film to surpass $1 billion and the second to cross $2 billion globally.

A rate is a ratio comparing two related quantities, specifically expressing how much of one quantity (the dependent variable) corresponds to a *single unit* of the other quantity (the independent variable). This makes a rate a measure of one quantity *per unit* of another quantity.

The main symbol of Christmas in Brazil is the Nativity scene (Presépio), which is very popular and serves as the primary decoration in many homes, churches, and public places. While Christmas trees are also decorated, the Nativity scene is highlighted as the central symbol. Other traditions include a late Christmas Eve dinner with specific foods like farofa, fried cod, and rabanada, accompanied by champagne or wine, often followed by attending Midnight Mass or watching the Pope's broadcast.

The term "prime minister" was first used in the 18th century in the United Kingdom. It was initially applied disparagingly by members of parliament to Sir Robert Walpole, implicitly comparing him to the powerful French minister Richelieu, which was not a compliment at the time due to Britain's rivalry with France.
Yuzuru Hanyu won the gold medal in men's single ice skating at the 2018 Winter Olympics.

Cain and Abel is a password recovery tool for Microsoft Windows. It recovers passwords by sniffing network packets, cracking password hashes using methods like dictionary attacks, brute force, and cryptanalysis (via rainbow tables).
Claudia Grace Wells played Jennifer Parker in the 1985 film *Back to the Future*.

To be inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame as a Performer, artists must first become eligible 25 years after their first record release. A nominating committee of historians then selects potential nominees. These nominees are voted on by roughly 500 experts worldwide (academics, journalists, producers, etc.), who evaluate the artists' influence and significance to rock and roll. To be inducted, an artist must receive more votes than any other nominee *and* secure over 50% of the total votes cast. Typically, only five to seven performers are inducted each year.
The 2012 Summer Olympics were held in London, United Kingdom, from 27 July to 12 August 2012.

The New Zealand flag features the Union Flag in the canton, symbolizing the country's historical links to Great Britain, and four red stars with white borders representing the Southern Cross constellation (Crux), signifying New Zealand's location in the South Pacific.
Jodie Sweetin portrayed Stephanie Judith Tanner, the middle sister, on *Full House*. Stephanie is the middle child of Danny and Pam, younger than D.J. and older than Michelle.
The first season of When Calls the Heart premiered in the United States on the Hallmark Channel on January 11, 2014.
Justice John Marshall Harlan II wrote the statement "one man's vulgarity is another's lyric" in his judicial opinion.

Tyrann Mathieu, an American football safety for the Houston Texans, is the player nicknamed the "Honey Badger". He earned this nickname during his college football career at Louisiana State University (LSU) due to his reputation for causing turnovers, specifically setting a Southeastern Conference record with 11 career forced fumbles.

The book *Are You My Mother?* is about a baby bird who hatches while his mother is away. Not knowing where she is, he sets off walking (since he can't fly) to find her. He asks a kitten, a hen, a dog, and a cow if they are his mother, but none of them are.

The purpose of the Bay of Pigs mission was to overthrow the increasingly communist government of Fidel Castro in Cuba. This was to be achieved by a CIA-trained and funded paramilitary group (Brigade 2506), composed primarily of Cuban exiles.

The people who escaped from Alcatraz were inmates Clarence Anglin, John Anglin, and Frank Morris. They broke out on the night of June 11 or early morning of June 12, 1962, using fake heads in their beds and an inflatable raft, and their fate remains uncertain. This is the only successful escape in the prison's history.

Donations collected through Trick-or-Treat for UNICEF primarily support UNICEF's global programming. In 2005, half of the proceeds were specifically allocated to aid victims of Hurricane Katrina within the United States, but otherwise, the funds go towards UNICEF's international efforts.
The inscription of Emma Lazarus's poem "The New Colossus" is found on a plaque mounted on the inner wall of the Statue of Liberty's pedestal, placed there in 1903.
In an autocracy, all important government decisions are made by one person who holds supreme power, subject to neither legal restraints nor regular popular control mechanisms.
The Earth orbits the Sun in a counterclockwise direction when viewed from a vantage point above the Earth's north pole.

Dwight Howard played for the Atlanta Hawks last year (during the 2016-2017 season). The passage details his signing with the Hawks on July 12, 2016, and describes his performance throughout that season, including the regular season and playoffs.
Neil Armstrong took the first steps on the moon on July 21, 1969, at 02:56:15 UTC. Buzz Aldrin joined him on the lunar surface about 20 minutes later.
Olivia Olson voices Marceline the Vampire Queen in most appearances on Adventure Time. Ava Acres voices her as a child, and Cloris Leachman voices her as an old woman.

England does not have a constitutionally protected right to bear arms; most handguns, automatic weapons, and centerfire semi-automatic weapons are generally illegal to possess without special permission.

The Great Depression in Canada started primarily due to the close economic ties with the United States. The 1929 New York Stock Market crash caused Americans to hoard money and reduce spending, leading to a US economic contraction that quickly spread to Canada. Additionally, manufacturing industries in Ontario and Quebec suffered from overproduction, causing massive lay-offs and bankruptcies, further deepening the crisis. While slightly less severe than the US collapse, Canada experienced the world's second sharpest economic downturn.

The D-Day landing beaches were five sectors along the Normandy coast: Utah Beach, Omaha Beach, Gold Beach, Juno Beach, and Sword Beach. Allied forces landed on these beaches on June 6, 1944, facing heavy fire, obstacles, and mines.
The fastest recorded Atlantic crossing by an ocean liner was achieved by the American ship *United States* in July 1952, taking 3 days, 10 hours, and 40 minutes to cross eastbound.
The Coral Sea merges with the Pacific Ocean along its eastern boundary, which is formed by Vanuatu and New Caledonia, and extends to the southern Solomon Islands in the northeast.

Ancient Egypt primarily used Egyptian hieroglyphs as its formal writing system. This system combined logographic, syllabic, and alphabetic elements, utilizing about 1,000 distinct characters. Cursive hieroglyphs were used for religious texts on papyrus and wood, while the later hieratic and demotic scripts were derived from hieroglyphs.
The Guinness Book of World Records was started by Sir Hugh Beaver, who conceived the idea, and was co-founded by brothers Norris and Ross McWhirter in London in August 1954.

President Richard Nixon proposed the establishment of the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and signed the executive order that created it, leading to the agency beginning operations on December 2, 1970.
Sheryl Crow sings the song "Soak Up the Sun", which was released as the lead single from her 2002 album *C'mon C'mon*.
The surname Edwards originated separately in both England and Wales as a patronymic surname, meaning "son of Edward".
Graham McTavish portrays the Saint of Killers in the television series adaptation of *Preacher*.

The Dred Scott v. Sandford case (1857) was a landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision where Dred Scott, an enslaved African American man, sued for his freedom after being taken to free states/territories. In a 7-2 ruling, Chief Justice Roger B. Taney wrote that Black people, whether enslaved or free, could not be U.S. citizens and therefore lacked standing to sue in federal court. The Court also ruled the federal government had no power to regulate slavery in federal territories. This decision denied Scott's freedom and was a significant factor leading to the Civil War.
Thomas Middleditch played Dwight's brother Jeb on The Office.

Corporal punishment was banned in South Africa through the Abolition of Corporal Punishment Act, which was passed in 1997 (Act No. 33 of 1997). This Act formally abolished judicial corporal punishment across South Africa.
The first permanent English colony in North America was located in Jamestown, Virginia.
Sierra Nevada Brewery opened its second facility, located on a forested tract adjacent to Asheville Regional Airport in Mills River, North Carolina, in early 2014.
The Bill of Rights, specifically the English Bill of Rights, received Royal Assent and became law on 16 December 1689.
People born on February 29 (leaplings) celebrate their birthday in non-leap years either on February 28, March 1, or they wait until the next actual February 29.
According to the 2016 census, the population of Prince Edward Island is 142,907 residents.

Bermuda is located in the North Atlantic Ocean. It is situated approximately 1,070 km east-southeast of Cape Hatteras, North Carolina (USA), 1,236 km south of Cape Sable Island, Nova Scotia (Canada), and 1,578 km north of Puerto Rico.

The buckeye is the mascot for Ohio State University. It was selected in 1965 because the buckeye is the official state tree of Ohio. The original costume was a papier-mâché nut worn by a student, which debuted at a football game on October 30, 1965. It was soon replaced by a fiberglass shell. The mascot was named "Brutus Buckeye" after a campus-wide contest won by student Kerry J. Reed later that year.

While originally written for The Emotions, the song "(If Loving You Is Wrong) I Don't Want to Be Right" has been performed by many singers. The most notable artist associated with the song is Luther Ingram, whose 1972 version was a major hit.

Anaerobic respiration allows living things to generate ATP (energy) when oxygen is unavailable. Like aerobic respiration, it uses reduced compounds (e.g., NADH) to create an electrochemical gradient (proton gradient) across a membrane. This gradient powers ATP synthase to produce ATP. The key difference is that anaerobic respiration uses a final electron acceptor other than oxygen (like sulfate or nitrate) to oxidize the reduced compounds and establish the necessary gradient. This provides an alternative energy-producing pathway in oxygen-deprived environments.

The national shift from lower drinking ages (like 18) to 21 was mandated by the National Minimum Drinking Age Act passed by Congress in 1984. This law required all states to raise their minimum purchase and public possession age to 21 by October 1986 or face a penalty. While some states had voluntarily raised their ages earlier (1976-1983), all 50 states and D.C. had complied with the age 21 mandate by mid-1988.

Imagine Dragons is an American rock band from Las Vegas, Nevada, consisting of members Dan Reynolds, Wayne Sermon, Ben McKee, and Daniel Platzman. They achieved significant success with albums like "Night Visions," "Smoke + Mirrors," and "Evolve," featuring hit singles such as "Radioactive," "Demons," "Believer," and "Thunder."

The cartilaginous rings in the trachea (C-shaped rings of hyaline cartilage) function to reinforce, protect, and maintain the airway. They reinforce the front and sides of the trachea, preventing it from collapsing during breathing and ensuring it stays open.

The tradition of gift-giving originated as a relic of pagan winter solstice celebrations, specifically the ancient Roman festival of Saturnalia held in December. When Christianity spread through the Roman Empire, the custom persisted. After December 25th was established as Jesus's birth date (around 336 AD), the practice was reinterpreted and incorporated into Christmas, tying it to the biblical story of the Magi giving gifts to baby Jesus and the legends surrounding Saint Nicholas (Santa Claus).

Recitative is a vocal style used in operas, oratorios, and cantatas that sits between singing and speaking; it adopts the rhythms of ordinary speech, does not repeat lines like formal songs, and resembles sung speech more than a structured musical composition.
Richard Armitage plays Thorin Oakenshield, the dwarf prince and leader (who becomes king) in Peter Jackson's film trilogy adaptation of *The Hobbit*.
The live-action/CG hybrid Woody Woodpecker movie was filmed in Canada during June and July 2016.
The highest criminal court in a district is the Sessions Court. This court is presided over by the District Judge.

A key economic effect of World War II was the unprecedented economic expansion in the United States. Spared from wartime devastation and with its industrial base largely intact and converted, the US emerged producing roughly half the world's industrial output. This advantageous position led to a historic boom, with US GDP soaring from $228 billion in 1945 to nearly $1.7 trillion by 1975.
Skiing halfpipe became an Olympic event at the 2014 Winter Games in Sochi, Russia, after being approved by the International Olympic Committee in April 2011.

Three Boston patriots hung the lanterns in the Old North Church steeple: Robert Newman (the church sexton), Captain John Pulling, and Thomas Bernard. Newman and Pulling each carried a lantern up to the steeple, while Bernard stood watch outside. They did this on Paul Revere's instructions to signal Charlestown patriots about British troop movements.
The last sustainable gray wolf pack in Yellowstone National Park was killed by 1926.

The Vikings first sailed to and reached Britain during the reign of King Beorhtric of Wessex (786-802 AD), when three ships of "Northmen" landed at Portland Bay in Dorset. This event predates the more famous first recorded *raid* on Lindisfarne in 793 AD.

The minimum dollar amount for federal court cases under diversity jurisdiction is $75,000, as specified in 28 U.S.C. § 1332(a). This requirement applies specifically to cases where the court's authority is based solely on the parties being citizens of different states.
Kiss sings "Rock and Roll All Nite". It was originally released on their 1975 album *Dressed to Kill* and became their signature song, often closing their concerts.

The belief in ancient Greek gods is called Hellenic polytheism. It involves worshiping the Olympians, nature divinities, underworld deities, and heroes, honoring ancestors, and making devotional offerings to the gods in exchange for blessings. Modern practitioners often base their ethics on ancient Greek virtues like reciprocity (kharis), hospitality, self-control, and piety (eusebeia), guided by moral codes such as the Delphic maxims.

In *Gotham by Gaslight*, the identity of Jack the Ripper is revealed to be Jacob Packer. Packer, who was educated using Thomas Wayne's money, became insane after Martha Wayne rejected his romantic advances. He began killing women who resembled Martha to silence the hallucinated sound of her laughter in his head. Batman discovers Packer's identity during a confrontation at the grave of Thomas and Martha Wayne.

In 3D computer graphics, 3D modeling is the process of creating a mathematical representation of an object's surface. This mathematical model (the 3D model) can then be used to generate a visual representation (a 2D image via 3D rendering) or serve as a representation within a computer simulation of physical processes.
Terrell Owens was elected to the Pro Football Hall of Fame in 2018, following controversy over his exclusion in his first two years of eligibility despite strong individual statistics.

The lymphatic vessels in the small intestine are called lacteals. They are the structures into which chylomicrons (packages of dietary fat) pass from the enterocytes. This entry forms a milky fluid called chyle, which the lacteals then transport towards the thoracic duct and ultimately into the bloodstream.
The inner sanctuary of the Parthenon where the statue of Athena was located is known as the naos.
The emblematic rhythms of the Dominican Republic are merengue and bachata, as these are the musical forms the country is mainly known for and are its most popular.

The governor is the nominal head of a state government in India, similar to the President at the national level. However, the real executive power resides with the chief minister, who leads the state's council of ministers. The governor's role is largely ceremonial, while the chief minister holds the actual governing authority.

The Easter lily (Lilium longiflorum) got its name because it is a symbol in Christianity of the resurrection of Jesus Christ, which is celebrated during the Easter season (Eastertide). This association stems from biblical references where Jesus mentions lilies, pious legends suggesting they grew where Jesus prayed in the Garden of Gethsemane, and their traditional use in churches throughout the Paschal season.
Season 2 of Limitless never came out because the series was cancelled by CBS after its first season (22 episodes) on May 25, 2016. It was not included in the network's 2016–2017 lineup.
The opening ceremony of the 2018 Winter Olympics was held on 9 February 2018.
Waylon Jennings sang (and wrote) the theme song for *The Dukes of Hazzard*, titled "Theme from The Dukes of Hazzard (Good Ol' Boys)".
The United States had approximately 1,281,900 active duty troops and an additional 801,200 reserve component personnel in fiscal year 2017.
John Hart played the Lone Ranger in the television series for one season, replacing Clayton Moore from 1952 until 1953.
Lin-Manuel Miranda and Jordan Fisher sing a duet version of "You're Welcome" over the Moana end credits.
Guy Boucher is the current head coach of the Ottawa Senators.

Sussex was formally divided into the two administrative counties of East Sussex and West Sussex in 1889 under the Local Government Act 1888. This division used boundaries (eastern and western divisions) that had been established earlier in 1832 after the Reform Act. While divisions existed before 1889, that year marks the creation of the distinct administrative counties.
The visible "smoke" rising from NYC manholes is actually condensation clouds, caused either by external water boiling on hot steam pipes or by leaks in the steam system itself.

"Suite: Judy Blue Eyes" was written by Stephen Stills about his former girlfriend, singer/songwriter Judy Collins. The title refers to her piercing blue eyes. The lyrics express Stills' thoughts about her and their imminent breakup, which he described as happening because they were "both too large for one house." Collins was even present when Stills recorded the demo tapes for the song.
Jack Scanlon played the character Shmuel, a Jewish inmate, in the Holocaust drama "The Boy in the Striped Pyjamas".

The Kansas City Royals defeated the New York Mets 4 games to 1 in the 2015 World Series. This means the series ended after only 5 games, making it the last World Series (as described in the passage) that did not go to a full 7 games.

Axons typically branch extensively ("very profusely"), and their end branches are specifically called telodendria. The swollen tip of each telodendron is known as the axon terminal, which forms synaptic connections with other neurons or cells. These axon terminals at the ends of the branches are one primary location where synapses occur.

Russia won the most medals at the 2014 Winter Olympics. Initially, they matched the Soviet Union's record of 13 gold medals, but later doping violations caused several medals to be stripped, allowing Norway to take the lead in the medal table. However, the Court of Arbitration for Sport reinstated some medals in early 2018, including 3 golds, returning Russia to the top position.

Each chamber of the US Congress determines its own rules. The passage explicitly states that the Senate establishes its Standing Rules, and this power (shared with the House of Representatives) comes directly from Article One, Section 5 of the US Constitution, which grants each House the authority to "determine the rules of its proceedings."

Las Vegas became a legal gambling town in 1931, following the Nevada state legislature's legalization of gambling at the local level that year. The first gambling license was issued to the Northern Club in 1931, and other casinos like the Las Vegas Club and Apache Hotel were soon licensed on Fremont Street, marking the start of its rise as a gaming center.

In Trivia Crack, Lives are used to start new games. Each time a player starts a new game (by pressing the New Game button) or accepts a Facebook friend's request to play, one Life is consumed. Lives act as a throttle on how often players can begin new matches and provide an opportunity for in-app purchases.
Alex Rodriguez, the New York Yankees' third baseman, was the highest paid player in Major League Baseball during the 2013 season with an annual salary of $29,000,000.

The tsunami generated by the magnitude 9.0–9.1 Great East Japan Earthquake (Tōhoku earthquake) on March 11, 2011, reached maximum heights of **up to 40.5 metres (133 feet) in Miyako, Iwate Prefecture**.

The triple threat position enables a player to shoot, pass, or dribble. When a player receives a pass and hasn't dribbled yet, they assume this stance (feet wide, knees bent, ball held ready). From this balanced position, they can immediately choose to jump-shoot, dribble (drive) past the defender, or pass to a teammate, making them a versatile offensive threat capable of all three actions.

The spinal cord becomes the cauda equina at its termination point. Due to differential growth between the spinal cord (which stops growing in infancy) and the vertebral column (which continues growing), the spinal cord ends at approximately the L1 or L2 vertebral level in adults (with normal variation from T12 to L3). At this termination point, the lower spinal nerve roots continue descending within the vertebral canal. These descending roots form the "bundle"-like structure known as the cauda equina, which extends caudally from the end of the spinal cord.
Clifford the Big Red Dog first aired in the US on PBS Kids on September 4, 2000. The UK version, with British voice actors, first aired on BBC 2 on April 1, 2002.
Kerris Dorsey plays Casey Beane, the daughter of Billy Beane (portrayed by Brad Pitt), in the film Moneyball.
In season 2 of Real Chance of Love ("Real Chance of Love 2: Back in the Saddle"), there were two winners: Ahmad (Real) chose Doll, and Kamal (Chance) chose Hot Wings.
Huntington's disease is named after the physician George Huntington, who provided a detailed description of the condition in 1872.

The House Rules Committee is vital to the legislative process because it determines the rules under which bills reach the House floor for debate and amendment, controlling the flow of legislation and acting as a powerful "traffic cop" for Congress.

France aided the United States primarily to gain revenge against Britain after its defeat in the Seven Years' War and to strategically weaken its rival, while also being motivated by ideological sympathy as the American Revolution was perceived in France as an Enlightenment struggle against British tyranny.

Helio Castroneves, a successful race car driver, won the fifth season of Dancing with the Stars with partner Julianne Hough. He later returned for the show's 15th season partnered with Chelsie Hightower but was eliminated early.
Bianca Ryan won the first season of America's Got Talent in 2006.
The Georgia State Panthers football team was founded in 2010.
In humans, the heart is located between the lungs in the middle compartment of the chest. It is a muscular organ that pumps blood throughout the body.
Nicole Gale Anderson plays the character Heather Chandler on The CW series *Beauty & the Beast*.

Chlorophyll in plants captures energy from the sun by utilizing photons within the 400–700 nm wavelength range, although significant energy losses occur throughout the photosynthetic process due to factors like incomplete absorption, photon energy degradation, conversion inefficiencies, and respiration.
Ozzie Smith has won the most Gold Glove Awards at shortstop, capturing 13 awards during his career with the St. Louis Cardinals.
Quarterback Don Majkowski wore number 7 for the Green Bay Packers after switching from number 5 following his rookie season in 1987.

The Maginot Line was a system of concrete fortifications, obstacles, and weapon installations built by France along its borders with Italy, Switzerland, Germany, and Luxembourg in the 1930s to prevent a German invasion.

The term "social role" refers to a set of interconnected expected behaviors, rights, obligations, beliefs, and norms associated with a specific social position or status within a given social situation; it represents how individuals in that position are generally expected to act and is a fundamental concept for understanding social behavior and society.
Abid Ali Neemuchwala became the CEO of Wipro in early 2016.

The passage explains that ATP hydrolysis, the removal of one phosphate group from ATP, results in the products adenosine diphosphate (ADP) and an inorganic phosphate (Pi). This reaction releases stored chemical energy used for biological work. ADP can undergo further hydrolysis to AMP.

Neptune is known as an "ice giant" because, while its atmosphere contains hydrogen and helium, its interior is primarily composed of ices (such as water, ammonia, and methane) and rock. This distinguishes it from gas giants like Jupiter and Saturn.

Fennec fox dens dug in compacted soils can require up to 120 square meters of space, featuring multiple chambers and up to 15 entrances. In softer sand, their dens are much simpler and smaller, typically consisting of a single chamber with one entrance.

The Gulf of Mexico basin contains a volume of roughly 660 quadrillion US gallons (or 550 quadrillion Imperial gallons) of water. This volume corresponds to approximately 2.5 million cubic kilometers. The basin itself has a surface area of about 1.6 million square kilometers.

Vasoepididymostomy is a surgical procedure that creates an anastomosis (connection) between a single epididymal tubule (with a luminal diameter of 0.15-0.25 mm) and the lumen of the vas deferens (diameter 0.3-0.4 mm). This surgery directly attaches the vas deferens to the epididymis above a point of obstruction, bypassing blockages within the epididymis or vas deferens caused by congenital issues, acquired conditions, or failed prior surgeries like vasovasostomy. It is performed specifically when sperm flow is blocked and a simpler vasovasostomy is not viable.

The Aqua-Lung, the first commercially successful open-circuit SCUBA apparatus, was invented in Paris during the winter of 1942–1943 by French engineer Émile Gagnan and French Naval Lieutenant Jacques Cousteau.

The name "Huey" originated from the helicopter's original military designation, HU-1, which people pronounced phonetically as "Huey." This nickname became so popular that Bell even cast it onto the helicopter's pedals, and it remained the common name even after the official designation changed to UH-1 in 1962.
The German High Seas Fleet was scuttled by its own crew at the Royal Navy's base in Scapa Flow, Scotland, in 1919 to prevent the ships from being seized by the Allied powers.
Kirsten Simone Vangsness is the actress who plays FBI Technical Analyst Penelope Garcia on the CBS drama series Criminal Minds.
Cozonac is a traditional Romanian sweet leavened bread. It is prepared for several major holidays in Romania, including Easter.
Betty Lynn played Thelma Lou, Deputy Barney Fife's girlfriend, on The Andy Griffith Show.

Executive producer SallyAnn Salsano hired casting director Doron Ofir to find the Jersey Shore cast. MTV executives like Van Toffler directed the search for individuals exhibiting candor, honesty, boldness, and a "combustible, chaotic mess" personality, rather than traditional beauty.
The bald eagle belongs to the genus *Haliaeetus*, commonly known as sea eagles.
William Wyler directed the 1946 American drama film *The Best Years of Our Lives*, which follows three United States servicemen readjusting to civilian life after World War II.

The notwithstanding clause (Section 33 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms) allows Parliament or provincial legislatures to override certain portions of the Charter. Its function is to provide a mechanism for these legislative bodies to bypass or suspend specific Charter rights when enacting laws.

Yes, Better Call Saul is explicitly a spin-off prequel to Breaking Bad. It follows small-time lawyer Jimmy McGill (later known as Saul Goodman) starting in 2002, six years before his appearance in Breaking Bad, though it also briefly explores events after the original series.

ICD-10-CM codes can have up to 7 characters. The structure is: Character 1-3 (disease category), Character 4 (etiology), Character 5 (body part), Character 6 (severity), and Character 7 (placeholder for increased specificity).
The pia mater is the meningeal layer that firmly adheres directly to the surface of the brain and spinal cord, following all of the brain's contours (gyri and sulci).

The first Governor-General of British India was Warren Hastings. Governors-General typically served five-year terms at the sovereign's pleasure and could be removed; a provisional replacement was sometimes appointed until a new one was chosen. Louis Mountbatten was the first Governor-General of independent India.
The heart of palm is the center core of the palm tree, found after the tree is cut down, the bark is removed, and the surrounding layers of white fibers are stripped away during processing.

The hymn "What a Friend We Have in Jesus" originated as a poem written by Joseph M. Scriven in 1855 to comfort his mother in Ireland while he was in Canada. Scriven published it anonymously and only received full credit in the 1880s. The tune most commonly associated with the hymn was composed by Charles Crozat Converse in 1868.

When the audience first meets Maria and Anita, they are working together in a bridal shop (where Anita is Bernardo's girlfriend and Maria's future sister-in-law). During this time, Maria confesses to Anita that she is not in love with Chino, the man she has come from Puerto Rico to marry, and Anita makes Maria a dress for the neighborhood dance.

The ceramic poppies installed in the Tower of London moat in 2014, titled "Blood Swept Lands and Seas of Red", were created by artist Paul Cummins, with the setting designed by Tom Piper. They commemorated the centenary of WWI, with each of the 888,246 poppies representing a British or Colonial serviceman killed in the war.

Jeff Healey's band was the house cover band for the bar featured in the movie *Road House*, starring Patrick Swayze. They performed in the film, with Healey having numerous acting scenes alongside Swayze, and also recorded songs for the movie's soundtrack while simultaneously working on their album *See the Light*.

The British army successfully invaded **Long Island, New York** in late August 1776. This large-scale amphibious landing involved 22,000 men and led to the Battle of Long Island on August 27th, where the British outflanked American defenses and drove them back. This invasion was part of General Howe's campaign to seize New York City, which ultimately resulted in the conquest of Manhattan Island, including the capture of Fort Washington and nearly 3,000 Continental troops.

Eddie Van Halen, the lead guitarist of Van Halen, wrote and performed the guitar solo for "Beat It" after being asked by Quincy Jones. He recorded it for free as a favor, using an amplifier borrowed from Allan Holdsworth.

While Arthur Chung was Guyana's first President (a ceremonial role) when it became a republic in 1970, a constitutional revision in 1980 transformed the presidency into an executive position. Forbes Burnham succeeded Chung and became President on October 6, 1980, making him the country's first executive president.
Uruguay hosted and won the inaugural World Cup, defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final.

Loose connective tissue, the most common connective tissue in vertebrates, explicitly includes adipose tissue, areolar tissue, and reticular tissue. It functions to hold organs in place, attach epithelial tissue to underlying layers, form connecting telae (like tela submucosa/subserosa), and surround blood vessels and nerves. Its structure features widely spaced cells, primarily fibroblasts (which secrete fibrous proteins and proteoglycans), embedded in a gelatinous extracellular matrix rich in collagenous and elastic fibers.

The IUPAC name for the element with atomic number 107 is bohrium. Initially assigned the temporary systematic name *unnilseptium* (symbol Uns), IUPAC recommended the name bohrium (not nielsbohrium) in 1994. Although the discoverers opposed it due to concerns about confusion with boron (especially the oxyanions bohrate and borate), the Danish branch of IUPAC approved bohrium, and it became the internationally recognized name in 1997. The names of the oxyanions remain unchanged despite their similar sound.
The Treasure of the Sierra Madre was written by B. Traven, a bilingual German author whose identity remains unknown. The novel was published in 1927.
The female lion character in *The Lion King* is called Nala. She is Simba's best friend and later wife, the mother of Kiara and Kion, and a prominent character in the original film.
The intersection of Highways 335 and 35 in Saskatchewan is noted as the location of the 2018 Humboldt Broncos bus crash, which resulted in 16 fatalities.
Dividends paid to shareholders are recorded as a cash outflow in the financing activities section of the cash flow statement.

The 180 degree line of longitude is called the 180th meridian or the antimeridian. It forms a circle with the Prime Meridian, dividing the Earth into the Western and Eastern Hemispheres. This line serves as the basis for the International Date Line, primarily because it traverses the open Pacific Ocean, although it also passes through Russia, Fiji, and Antarctica.
